questions.specification:
Denmark is a highly developed economy in Northern Europe with a population of about 5.9 million. It boasts one of the highest standards of living in the world, supported by a broad-based welfare system and progressive taxation. The service sector, advanced manufacturing, and renewable-energy technology form key parts of its economy. Denmark’s central bank has maintained low interest rates, helping to foster economic stability. However, challenges such as ensuring long-term sustainability of the welfare system and addressing potential future energy shortfalls remain.

Exports are crucial for Denmark’s economic success, with wind turbines, pharmaceuticals, and meat products contributing significantly to export revenues. Wind turbine manufacturers in Denmark have become global leaders, but recent shifts in global demand and competition from Asian producers have tested the capacity of local firms. Meanwhile, a high rate of personal and corporate income tax has provided funds for extensive public expenditure, including education, healthcare, and infrastructure.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Denmark (2022–2023)

| Indicator                                  | 2022          | 2023 (est.)   |
|--------------------------------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Nominal GDP (billion DKK)                 | 2,350         | 2,485         |
| Real GDP growth rate (%)                  | 2.1           | 3.0           |
| Inflation rate (%)                        | 5.5           | 2.8           |
| Unemployment rate (%)                     | 4.5           | 4.2           |
| Government spending (billion DKK)         | 860           | 920           |
| Marginal propensity to consume (MPC)      | 0.8           | 0.8           |
| Gini coefficient (after taxes/transfers)  | 0.27          | 0.26          |

Table 2: Market for Wind Turbines Produced in Denmark

| Price per turbine (DKK) | Quantity Demanded (units per year) | Quantity Supplied (units per year) |
|-------------------------|-------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| 10,000,000              | 100                                 |  60                                  |
| 11,000,000              | 90                                  |  65                                  |
| 12,000,000              | 84                                  |  68                                  |
| 15,000,000              | 75                                  |  72                                  |

Table 3: Income Distribution in Denmark (2022)

| Income Group         | Income Range (DKK/year) | Proportion of Households (%) |
|----------------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|
| Lowest 20 %          | 0 – 240,000            | 20                           |
| Second 20 %          | 240,001 – 400,000      | 20                           |
| Third 20 %           | 400,001 – 600,000      | 20                           |
| Fourth 20 %          | 600,001 – 850,000      | 20                           |
| Highest 20 %         | Above 850,000          | 20                           |

Table 4: Overview of Tax Rates in Denmark

| Tax Category            | Rate (% of taxable income / value) |
|-------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Corporate income tax    | 22                                 |
| Top personal income tax | 52                                 |
| Standard VAT rate       | 25                                 |
| Reduced VAT rate        | 15                                 |

In 2023, an energy-infrastructure firm in Denmark is planning a major investment worth 1.2 billion DKK, and claims it could significantly boost the economy by generating additional consumption expenditures.

Wind Turbine Exports  
Denmark exported 70 units of wind turbines at an average price of 11,000,000 DKK per unit to other European countries in 2022. Owing to rising demand for renewable energy worldwide, the price of wind turbines exported from Denmark is expected to rise from 11,000,000 DKK to 12,000,000 DKK per unit in 2023. However, global competition from producers in Asia might cause changes in the quantity demanded.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the real GDP growth (in billion DKK) from 2022 to 2023 for Denmark.
parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Based on Table 2, estimate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for wind turbines in Denmark when the price increases from 11,000,000 DKK to 12,000,000 DKK per turbine.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate percentage change in quantity supplied: ((68 − 65) / 65) × 100 ≈ 4.62 %.  
• Calculate percentage change in price: ((12 − 11) / 11) × 100 ≈ 9.09 %.  
• PES = 4.62 % / 9.09 % ≈ 0.51.

parts.content: Using information from the text and Table 4, explain one way in which Denmark’s progressive tax system (top personal tax rate of 52 %) may help reduce income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Higher-income earners pay a larger proportion of their earnings in taxes, reducing their disposable income relative to lower earners.  
• This generates government revenue that can be redistributed through extensive public services, such as education and healthcare.  
• Redistribution of income through such services narrows the income gap between high-income and low-income groups.  
• The Gini coefficient is lower after taxes and transfers, consistent with Denmark’s progressive tax approach.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of Denmark could implement to increase its long-term economic growth, while ensuring external competitiveness.
parts.markscheme:
• Long-term economic growth is a sustained increase in real output over time. External competitiveness involves the ability of domestic firms to compete with foreign producers in global markets.  
• A supply-side policy that invests in research and development (R&D) for renewable-energy technology could enhance productivity, given Denmark’s strong wind turbine sector and advanced manufacturing.  
• Higher productivity lowers average costs of production, which potentially leads to reduced prices of exports, maintaining competitiveness against Asian firms.  
• Funding for specialized training in renewable-energy engineering would improve labor quality, raising the country’s productive capacity and shifting the long-run aggregate supply curve to the right. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/69461cd8-ecb7-446f-9901-b8993cc4a78a)
• Adopting targeted tax incentives for firms investing in new technology could encourage private sector research that complements government programs.  
• The policy would utilize Denmark’s existing corporate tax structure by offering partial exemptions or deductions for R&D expenditure, incentivizing innovation and keeping company costs manageable.  
• Improved technology in wind turbine production strengthens export revenues and diversifies the economy, which mitigates risks from shifts in global demand.  
• Greater future output from a more innovative renewable-energy sector addresses concerns about energy shortfalls, while increased employment in R&D contributes to higher incomes throughout the economy.  
• The policy may face opportunity costs if funds are diverted from welfare or infrastructure, yet Denmark’s broad-based welfare system and relatively high government spending suggest capacity for redirected investment.  
• Embedding cost-effective R&D incentives, paired with education and infrastructure, helps sustain economic growth and preserve Denmark’s export edge in a globally competitive market.

questions.specification:
Fiji is an archipelago located in the South Pacific, known for its thriving tourism industry and longstanding sugar sector. Tourism directly and indirectly accounts for nearly 38% of Fiji’s gross domestic product (GDP), making it one of the country’s main sources of foreign exchange. The island nation receives over 800 000 international visitors in a normal (non-pandemic) year, with most tourists arriving from Australia and New Zealand. However, dependence on tourism also makes Fiji vulnerable to external shocks such as global economic downturns or natural disasters.

The sugar industry is the second-largest contributor to Fiji’s export earnings, employing workers in growing, harvesting, and processing sugarcane. Due to changing weather patterns and competition from other sugar-producing nations, sugar production in Fiji faces challenges in expanding supply. In an effort to diversify government revenue, Fiji applies a 9% value added tax (VAT) on domestic sugar sales.

In 2022, the Fijian government announced a 200 million FJD infrastructure investment program aimed at improving rural roads, upgrading port facilities, and modernizing sugar processing plants. Economists estimate Fiji’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) at 0.75, suggesting a potentially significant boost to aggregate demand if the infrastructure spending is effectively implemented.

**Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Fiji (2019–2020)**

| Indicator                      |   2019  |   2020  |
|--------------------------------|--------:|--------:|
| Real GDP (FJD millions)        |  11 500 |  11 845 |
| Population (thousands)         |     889 |     895 |
| Inflation rate (%)             |     1.5 |     1.0 |
| Gini coefficient               |    0.37 |    0.36 |

**Table 2: Sugar Market Data in Fiji**

| Year | Price (FJD/ton) | Quantity Demanded (million tons) | Quantity Supplied (million tons) |
|-----:|----------------:|---------------------------------:|---------------------------------:|
| 2021 |             800 |                             1.20 |                             1.05 |
| 2022 |             840 |                             1.10 |                             1.04 |

Additional Information  

• Fiji’s VAT on sugar is 9%.  
• The government’s total planned infrastructure investment in 2022 is 200 million FJD.  
• Economists estimate Fiji’s MPC = 0.75.  
• Corporate tax rate is 20%.  
• Personal income tax is a progressive system up to 20%.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for sugar in Fiji when the price increases from 800 FJD per ton in 2021 to 840 FJD per ton in 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(1.10 – 1.20) / 1.20] × 100 = –8.33%  
• Percentage change in price = [(840 – 800) / 800] × 100 = 5%  
• PED = (–8.33%) / (5%) = –1.67

parts.content: Using information from the text above, calculate the total change in real GDP resulting from the government’s 200 million FJD infrastructure investment, given the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) of 0.75.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 / (1 – MPC) = 1 / (1 – 0.75) = 4  
• Total change in GDP = 4 × 200 million FJD = 800 million FJD

parts.content: Using data from Table 1, explain how a reduction in the Gini coefficient might benefit Fiji’s long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• The Gini coefficient in Fiji decreased from 0.37 to 0.36, indicating a decrease in income inequality.  
• Lower inequality can help increase access to education and health for lower-income groups, raising the overall productivity of the labor force.  
• Increased purchasing power among a broader base of consumers can stimulate domestic demand and investment.  
• Greater social cohesion and reduced social tensions can support a stable environment for long-term economic growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the government of Fiji in order to reduce the country’s vulnerability to external shocks arising from tourism and sugar exports.
parts.markscheme:
• Diversification is the process of broadening the range of economic activities to reduce reliance on a narrow set of industries.  
• External shocks are unforeseeable events that can disrupt exports of goods or services, causing unstable growth and foreign exchange earnings.  
• A supply-side policy such as providing tax incentives for new manufacturing or agricultural industries would help create additional export sectors beyond tourism and sugar.  
• Implementing targeted subsidies for developing alternative crops or supporting high-value agribusiness could spread risk and stabilize national income when global tourism demand falls or sugar prices decline. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4a69fc4a-092a-4091-a603-4b1d789c4fd3)
• Greater investment in infrastructure, already highlighted by the 200 million FJD program, can be directed toward supporting logistics, transport, and technology that benefit multiple sectors.  
• A broadened production base can ensure that abrupt falls in tourist arrivals or sugar export revenue are offset by other forms of export earnings.  
• Reliance on robust domestic and regional markets may reduce susceptibility to global economic downturns or natural disasters affecting tourism and sugarcane yields.  
• Improved human capital through training programs and research grants can boost innovation in new industries, increasing the country’s resilience.  
• Drawing from the case study, such policies lower the risk of sudden falls in GDP and foreign exchange receipts, mitigate high unemployment during tourism slumps, and ensure stable government revenue.  
• Over time, a more diverse economy would maintain steady growth, support inclusive development, and reduce long-term vulnerability to global shocks.

questions.specification:
Serbia is a country located in Southeastern Europe with a population of approximately 6.9 million. Its official currency is the Serbian dinar (RSD). According to official estimates, Serbia’s nominal GDP in 2022 reached US\$65.2 billion, growing from US$63.5 billion in 2021. The unemployment rate stands at 9.8%, although it can be higher in rural areas. Inflation in 2022 averaged about 11.5%, driven partly by increasing energy prices.

Serbia is well-known for its agricultural output—especially raspberries, where it ranks among the top producers in the world. Domestically, the government charges a 20% value added tax (VAT) on many goods and services. The personal income tax system has a base rate of 10%, rising gradually for higher income brackets, while corporate income is taxed at 15%. The Gini coefficient stands at 0.35, suggesting moderate income inequality.

Trade is a constant focus of Serbian economic policy. Serbia has strong trade links with the European Union (EU), which accounts for roughly 63% of its exports. Policymakers have debated using expansionary fiscal policy to support economic growth, focusing on infrastructure development and public investment.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Serbia (2022)  
| Indicator               | Value          |
|-------------------------|----------------|
| Population             | 6.9 million    |
| Nominal GDP (US$)      | 65.2 billion   |
| GDP in 2021 (US$)      | 63.5 billion   |
| Unemployment rate      | 9.8%           |
| Inflation rate         | 11.5%          |
| Gini coefficient       | 0.35           |

Table 2: Labour Market Data for Serbia (2022)  
| Population Over 15 (millions) | Employed (millions) | Unemployed (millions) |
|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 5.0                            | 3.5                  | 0.38                   |

Table 3: Domestic Market for Raspberries in Serbia  
| Price (RSD per kg) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (tonnes) |
|--------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| 300                | 680,000                   | 640,000                    |
| 350                | 620,000                   | 680,000                    |

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the unemployment rate in Serbia for 2022.
parts.markscheme:
- Sum of labour force = 3.5 million (employed) + 0.38 million (unemployed) = 3.88 million.  
- Unemployment rate = (Unemployed ÷ Labour force) × 100 = (0.38 ÷ 3.88) × 100 ≈ 9.8%.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate the approximate real GDP growth rate from 2021 to 2022 for Serbia.
parts.markscheme:
- Nominal GDP in 2021: US$63.5 billion; Nominal GDP in 2022: USD 65.2 billion.  
- Nominal GDP growth rate ≈ [(65.2 − 63.5) ÷ 63.5] × 100 ≈ 2.68%.  
- Approximate real GDP growth rate ≈ 2.68% - 11.5% (inflation) ≈ −8.82%.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in government infrastructure spending might affect Serbia’s real output.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/88adff2c-c0cd-413a-aded-f7c3f161530f)

- Increasing government spending shifts the aggregate demand curve to the right (AD1 -> AD2), raising the equilibrium level of real output from Y to Yp.  
- This can also push up the price level (pl1 -> pl2) if the economy is near full capacity.  
- If spare capacity exists (Real GDP (Y) is lower than Yp), real output can rise significantly with limited inflationary pressure.  
- The overall impact depends on the shape of the AS curve and the size of the multiplier effect.

- New Gini coefficient ≈ 0.35 − (0.14 × 0.35) = 0.35 − 0.049 = 0.301

parts.content: Using information from the text, explain how continuing inflation at 11.5% might impact real wages in Serbia.
parts.markscheme:
- Prolonged inflation at 11.5% tends to erode the purchasing power of wages if nominal wages do not increase at the same rate.  
- Workers experience a reduction in real income and can afford fewer goods and services.  
- Firms may face pressure to raise wages, which could increase production costs and potentially feed into further price increases.  
- High inflation can lead to uncertainty and reduced consumer confidence, unless wages keep pace with price changes.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the government of Serbia in order to reduce the unemployment rate.
parts.markscheme:
- Unemployment refers to the people in the labour force who are actively seeking work but remain jobless, and an unemployment rate of 9.8% indicates a significant portion of the population without jobs.  
- Expansionary fiscal policy can shift aggregate demand outward, potentially creating new employment opportunities; this aligns with the text mentioning infrastructure development and public investment.  
- Increased government spending on roads, public transport, and other infrastructure projects can employ more workers directly and stimulate private sector activity, especially in rural areas with higher unemployment.  
- The Keynesian multiplier suggests that an initial injection of government spending can lead to a larger increase in overall income, boosting consumption and further reducing unemployment through higher aggregate demand.  
- Stronger public investment may attract additional foreign direct investment (FDI) if improved infrastructure raises productivity, which can create complementary private-sector jobs and support international trade links.  
- Robust infrastructure can benefit the agricultural sector, including raspberries, by reducing transportation costs and enhancing competitiveness, thereby sustaining jobs in rural regions.  
- The diagram displays a rightward shift of the aggregate demand curve from AD1 to AD2, driven by increased government spending on infrastructure. This shift raises real GDP (Y -> Yp), increases price level (pl1 -> pl2) and lowers unemployment as higher demand for goods and services boosts production and job creation. The multiplier effect amplifies this impact, while idle capacity in the economy allows for non-inflationary growth—highlighting how fiscal stimulus can address Serbia’s 9.8% unemployment rate.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/88adff2c-c0cd-413a-aded-f7c3f161530f)

- A risk involves higher government debt or potential inflationary pressures if the economy nears full capacity; however, Serbia’s current GDP figures and existing unemployment suggest room for output expansion.  
- Improving workforce skills alongside infrastructure spending through targeted training programs can reduce structural unemployment in sectors that demand new skills, helping long-term employment stability.  
- Balancing the potential budget deficit against the social advantages of lower unemployment may require complementary measures, such as slight adjustments in tax structures or careful prioritization of projects.  
- This policy targets unemployment directly, supports growth in key industries highlighted in the case study, and may lead to a more equitable distribution of income if sustained over time.

questions.specification:
Italy is the Eurozone’s third largest economy. Although it experienced a sharp contraction in 2020 due to the global pandemic, economic recovery followed in 2021 and 2022, partly driven by an upturn in manufacturing and consumer spending. Italy’s unemployment rate, however, remains higher than the European Union average. Income inequality, measured by the Gini coefficient, is moderate but still a concern for policymakers aiming to promote inclusive growth.

Italian coffee culture remains a vital part of domestic consumption. Table 2 provides data for the market for coffee beans. Despite fluctuations in demand and price, Italy’s coffee imports and exports of processed coffee products continue to be important for the country’s trade balance.

In 2021, Italy’s exports of manufactured goods such as machinery and vehicles led to a small trade surplus, as shown in Table 3. The government also relies on progressive income taxes to finance public spending. Some economists argue that a well-designed policy could improve both growth prospects and income distribution.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for coffee beans when the price increases from €9 to €10 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
- PED = (percentage change in quantity demanded) ÷ (percentage change in price)  
- Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(29 − 32) ÷ 32] × 100 = −9.375% (approximately)  
- Percentage change in price = [(10 − 9) ÷ 9] × 100 = 11.11% (approximately)  
- PED = (−9.375% ÷ 11.11%) ≈ −0.84 (inelastic demand; value ≈ 0.84 when ignoring the sign)

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Italian government to promote more equitable economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
- A recommended policy is an increase in government spending on education, financed through existing progressive income taxes.  
- This policy targets both short-term and long-term economic growth by increasing aggregate demand (AD) through higher government spending and improving long-term productive capacity (LRAS) by enhancing human capital.
- While the direct cause is on Aggregate Demand, over time, LRAS also shifts rightward (from LRAS₁ to LRAS₂) as human capital improves, boosting productivity and potential output. This dual shift illustrates how education investment in Italy can stimulate growth, reduce unemployment, and narrow income inequality by making the workforce more skilled and competitive in key export sectors.  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4fb804f8-be4e-4131-82f1-76e61bbfacde)

- A reference to the moderate Gini coefficient (0.33–0.34) indicates that income distribution remains a concern for policymakers, and further investment in education can help narrow income gaps over time.  
- An improvement in skill levels boosts productivity in sectors such as machinery and vehicles (main export goods), which strengthens Italy’s trade surplus while reducing unemployment from the current 8.9–9.5% range.  
- Income inequality can be alleviated by ensuring that formerly disadvantaged groups, who might otherwise remain unemployed, gain relevant skills and access better-paying jobs.  
- The increased government spending on education may add to the budget burden, but it can be partially offset by the progressive tax system that raises revenue more from higher-income earners, reducing regressive effects.  
- A more equitable distribution of earnings could effectively raise aggregate consumption and investment, supporting Italy’s recovery from the recent economic contraction and sustaining growth momentum.  
- A better-educated labor force enhances competitiveness of Italian exports (machinery, vehicles, food and beverage), cultivating higher value-added production and stabilizing the external balance.  
- The policy may take time to implement and demonstrate results, but it addresses the structural causes of income inequality (skills and access to opportunity) rather than relying on short-term measures.  
- Overall, this approach potentially boosts Italy’s real GDP over time, lowers the unemployment rate, and narrows the income gap, moving the economy toward wider economic prosperity.

parts.content: Explain the concept of price elasticity of supply (PES) and how it is calculated.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 

### Definitions  
- **Price Elasticity of Supply (PES):** The responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in a product’s price, measured by the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price.  

### Economic Theory 
- **Formula and Interpretation**  
  - The PES for a good is calculated through the following formula:

$$\text{PES} = \frac{\%\Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}$$

  - If PES = 1, supply is unit elastic (proportionate change in quantity supplied matches the proportionate change in price).  
  - If PES > 1, supply is price elastic.  
  - If PES < 1, supply is price inelastic. 

- **Impact of PES on Supply Curves**
	- Supply curves reflect the value of the PES.
	- Unit elastic supply curves intercept the axes at the intersection of the axis.
	- Elastic supply curves intercept the axes at the price axis, and are relatively flat.
	- Inelastic supply curves intercept the axes at the quantity axis, and are relatively steep.
	- This can be seen in the figures below.

### Diagram

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9ae3afe5-3af3-4cee-a7cf-d3c02e8b4ec2)

(Labels must show how a move from price P₁ to P₂ causes a larger shift in Q on the elastic supply curve compared to the smaller shift on the inelastic supply curve. If not, correct axes scales are also valid.)

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate tradable permits as a measure to reduce environmental damage.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition  
- **Tradable permits**: A legal cap set by the government, limiting the amount of emissions that can be emitted. Each firm are provided a set of permits, which can be exchanged with other firms.  

### Economic Theory  
- Goods or services whose production leads to environmental damage have negative production externalities.
- This is because they have spillover costs passed on to third parties, from their production, not reflected in their market price.
- Without intervention, the market equilibrium occurs where the marginal private cost (MPC) curve intersects the marginal private benefit (MPB) curve.  
- The social optimal output is lower because the true cost to society is higher, captured by the marginal social cost (MSC) curve, which lies above MPC.  
- This can be seen in the figure below. 
- The free market produces MPC=MPB ($Q_m$).
- However, the socially optimum quantity ($Q_{opt} that achieves allocative efficiency is MSC = MSB.
- Therefore, it can be seen how negative production externalities lead to overproduction and welfare loss.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/753fc371-f81e-407d-8841-06bdbb5f1569)

- Tradable permits address this by:  
  - Setting a cap on the total level of emissions (e.g., total carbon emissions). This creates a fixed vertical supply. 
  - Distributing permits to firms, each representing the right to emit a specific amount.  
  - Allowing firms that can reduce pollution at lower cost to sell their unused permits to firms with higher abatement costs.  
  - Encouraging investment in cleaner technology because reducing emissions frees up permits to sell for profit. 
  - The figure below illustrates the market for tradable permits.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2d5bb1e8-4b55-415d-a3e5-6b3e8c5c4e74)

  - The profit motive driven by tradable permits shifts the supply curve upward for firms that must purchase additional permits, internalizing the external cost into their production decisions.
  - As a result, the market can reduce its production to the socially optimum level.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a7b40e30-5eca-4169-b562-8162cd923468)

- Over time, the government can reduce the cap, driving the permit price up and further incentivizing emission reductions. 


### Evaluation  
- **Short-run impacts**:  
  - Firms face higher costs if they cannot reduce emissions easily; some may pass these costs on to consumers.  
  - Government earns revenue from auctioning permits, potentially reinvesting it in greener technologies.
  - It is difficult for governments to set an appropriate amount of tradable permits, lower than current levels of pollution.
  - There are costs in regulating and tracking emissions for the government, which have opportunity costs.
  - Society experiences less environmental damage, but certain industries might lose competitiveness if permit costs are high.  
- **Long-run impacts**:  
  - Encourages innovation and cleaner technology as firms strive to reduce permit expenses.  
  - Potential restructuring of industries toward less pollutive processes, leading to more sustainable economic growth.  
  - If the cap is tightened gradually, overall emissions continue to decline, benefiting societal welfare.
- **Real-world examples**  
	- The **European Union Emissions Trading System (EU ETS)** introduced a cap-and-trade scheme for greenhouse gas emissions.  
		- Data from the European Environment Agency indicates an approximate **8% decrease** in emissions from the power and manufacturing sectors between 2013 and 2020.  
		- Firms investing in cleaner energy sources sold surplus permits, creating a financial incentive for ongoing emission reductions.  
	- The **US Acid Rain Program** in the 1990s addressed sulfur dioxide emissions.  
		- By 2010, sulfur dioxide emissions had declined by about **50%** compared to 1980 levels.  
		- Permit trading was credited with lowering compliance costs significantly below initial estimates, while improving air quality.  
	- Some issues arose when permit prices fell too low, reducing the incentive to adopt clean technology (seen in certain phases of the EU ETS).  
	- Concerns include possible manipulation if large firms hoard permits or if the initial permit allocation is too generous, undermining environmental goals.

## Conclusion  
- Tradable permits can be an effective, market-based method to internalize the external costs of pollution and reduce environmental damage.  
- Policy success depends on setting a stringent cap, ensuring transparent permit allocation, and maintaining oversight to prevent manipulation.  
- With appropriate safeguards and progressive tightening of the cap, tradable permits can steer economies toward lower emissions and greater environmental sustainability while encouraging innovation.

questions.specification:
Austria is a landlocked country in Central Europe with a population of approximately 9 million. It has a high-income economy, diversified across manufacturing, services, and a thriving tourism sector. During 2022, tourism alone contributed about 15% of Austria’s gross domestic product (GDP), fueled by both winter sports and year-round cultural tourism.

Austria’s real GDP growth rate moderately improved between 2019 and 2022, assisted by strong consumer demand, increasing trade with neighboring economies (most notably Germany), and an expansionary fiscal stance. Government spending on infrastructure has grown, although policy makers closely watch any inflationary pressures that could arise from such fiscal expansion.

Despite relatively low inequality levels compared to many other developed nations, Austria’s Gini coefficient has inched upward from 0.26 a decade ago to around 0.28 in 2022. Debates on whether to reform personal income tax rates or strengthen social welfare programs have gained attention.

In terms of taxation, Austria uses a progressive personal income tax system with a top marginal rate of 55%. Corporate income taxes for resident firms currently stand at 25%, although there have been proposals to reduce this rate to 22%. To stimulate the economy further, the government allocated €5 billion in 2022 toward infrastructure projects, anticipating that the Keynesian multiplier would raise overall economic activity significantly.

Table 1 below shows simplified data on the Austrian ski package market, while Tables 2–4 provide additional macroeconomic and distribution information that will be referred to in the questions.

---

Table 1: The Market for Austrian Ski Packages (2021–2022)

| Year | Average Price per Package (EUR) | Quantity Demanded (millions) | Quantity Supplied (millions) |
|------|---------------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 2021 | 900                             | 4.0                          | 3.8                          |
| 2022 | 960                             | 3.6                          | 4.0                          |

---

Table 2: Nominal GDP and Price Index in Austria (2019–2022)

| Year | Nominal GDP (billion EUR) | Price Index (2015=100) |
|------|---------------------------|-------------------------|
| 2019 | 390                       | 104                     |
| 2020 | 382                       | 105                     |
| 2021 | 400                       | 106                     |
| 2022 | 425                       | 108                     |

---

Table 3: Selected Economic Indicators (2022)

| Government Infrastructure Spending (billion EUR) | Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) | Proposed Corporate Tax Rate (%) | Current Personal Income Tax Rate (progressive, top rate) |
|--------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| 5                                                | 0.75                                 | 25                              | 55%                                                       |

---

Table 4: Income Distribution Data in Austria (2022)

| Income Group                  | Average Annual Income (EUR) | Population Share (%) |
|------------------------------|-----------------------------|----------------------|
| Highest quintile (Q5)        | 65,000                      | 20                   |
| Fourth quintile (Q4)         | 50,000                      | 20                   |
| Third quintile (Q3)          | 35,000                      | 20                   |

parts.content: Define the term “Keynesian multiplier.”
parts.markscheme: • The Keynesian multiplier is the factor by which an initial injection of spending leads to a proportionally larger increase in aggregate demand and total national income.

parts.content: Explain why an economy that relies heavily on tourism might encounter risks to its macroeconomic stability.
parts.markscheme:
• A large share of output and employment depends on tourist arrivals, which can fluctuate due to global economic conditions or unexpected events.  
• External shocks (such as pandemics or geopolitical tensions) can sharply reduce tourist flows, generating volatility in aggregate demand.  
• Seasonal demand leads to uneven expenditure patterns throughout the year, complicating fiscal and monetary policy planning.  
• Heavy reliance on one sector can reduce diversification, making the economy more vulnerable to downturns in the tourism industry.

- Ratio = 65,000 ÷ 15,000 = 4.33
- Income ratio: 4.3 (or 4.33)

parts.content: Using information from Table 4 (and the text above), explain how Austria’s relatively low Gini coefficient might influence its long-term economic growth and social outcomes.
parts.markscheme:
• A lower Gini coefficient indicates more equitable income distribution, which can enhance social cohesion and potentially reduce social tensions.  
• More equitable incomes can enable broader consumer spending, supporting aggregate demand across multiple sectors.  
• Higher social mobility and smaller income gaps may improve human capital development, as more people can afford education and healthcare.  
• Over the long term, these social outcomes can strengthen productivity and economic performance, supporting sustainable expansion.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the Austrian government could implement to diversify its economy beyond tourism while ensuring sustainable, long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• Diversification refers to expanding the range of economic activities so that the national economy is less vulnerable to shocks in a single sector.  
• Sustainable economic growth involves meeting current economic needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs.  
• The policy could involve targeted subsidies and regulatory support for high-value manufacturing and “green” technology sectors, prompted by the government’s desire to reduce reliance on tourism and foster resilience.  
• Lowering the corporate tax rate further or providing tax credits for research and development may incentivize firms to invest in advanced manufacturing, potentially complementing the existing manufacturing base.  
• This supply-side measure can shift the long-run aggregate supply curve rightward, raising Austria’s productive capacity and reducing the risk of overdependence on seasonal tourist demand.  
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/34602344-d49f-4868-a22a-0d4e91421cf1)
• Larger investment inflows, drawn by a more favorable corporate climate, can generate positive externalities such as technology spillovers and a more skilled workforce.  
• Data showing strong GDP growth and high government infrastructure spending suggests that additional support for technology clusters can build on existing public investment to stimulate further economic activity and employment.  
• Reallocating some of the €5 billion in infrastructure spending toward modern transport links, digital networks, and research facilities can complement private investment, ensuring a balanced approach between public and private sectors.  
• A relatively low Gini coefficient, as indicated in the data, implies that policies targeting new industries could further spread income benefits and maintain social stability.  
• This approach can strengthen long-term economic growth, reduce macroeconomic vulnerability, and allow Austria to maintain a balanced economic structure that does not rely solely on tourism.

parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition:

- **Price Elasticity of Supply (PES)**: Measures the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price, calculated as the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price.

- **Unused Capacity**: The extent to which a firm's available resources (such as machinery and labor) are not being utilized in production.

### Economic Theory:

- When a firm has significant unused capacity (idle machinery, unproductive labor), they can increase production quickly if price rises.

- This higher ability to expand output without incurring substantial additional costs tends to make the supply of the product more elastic.

- In contrast, when a firm operates near or at full capacity, any increase in production requires the acquisition of more capital or labor, which takes time and adds expense.

- This difficulty in increasing output causes the supply to be more inelastic.

- The degree of unused capacity directly affects the slope of the supply curve:

- Substantial unused capacity => more elastic supply curve (relatively flatter).

- Little or no unused capacity => more inelastic supply curve (relatively steeper).

**For firms with limited capacity:**
- In the short run, even if price increases, a firm with limited capacity cannot rapidly expand output, contributing to inelastic supply.

- In the long run, firms can invest in more machinery, hire additional labor, and thus create or restore unused capacity, making supply more elastic over time.

### Diagram:

- A diagram can illustrate two supply curves: one flatter (with unused capacity, more elastic) and one steeper (with minimal unused capacity, more inelastic).

- Both curves originate from a similar price range, but the flatter curve shows a greater change in quantity supplied for a given price change.

- Correct axes labels (y-axis for price, x-axis for Quantity)

parts.content: Explain how the rate at which costs increase with output affects the elasticity of supply.
parts.markscheme:
### Definition:

- Price Elasticity of Supply (PES): A measure of the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price, calculated as the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price.

- Marginal Cost: The additional cost incurred in producing one more unit of output.

- Diminishing Marginal Returns: In the short run, adding more of a variable factor (e.g., labor) to a fixed factor (e.g., capital) eventually causes the additional output to rise at a decreasing rate, making production more costly per extra unit.

### Economic Theory:

- When the rate at which costs increase with output is low, firms can more easily and inexpensively expand production in response to higher prices.

- As costs stay relatively stable, the gap between price and marginal cost remains larger when price rises.

- Producers have an incentive to increase output more substantially, resulting in a more elastic supply (PES > 1).

- When the rate at which costs increase with output is high, each additional unit produced becomes more expensive.

- Expansion of output raises marginal cost significantly.

- Producers are less willing or able to increase output in response to price changes, leading to a more inelastic supply (PES < 1).

- The law of diminishing marginal returns in the short run contributes to higher marginal costs, because beyond a certain point, each additional unit of a variable factor adds less extra output.

- As diminishing marginal returns set in, total costs rise at a faster rate, lowering the willingness or ability of firms to expand production in response to price changes.

- The short-run supply curve will often become steeper, reflecting greater inelasticity.

- In the long run, fixed factors of production can be increased, and economies of scale might reduce average cost.

- If larger-scale production techniques keep cost increases low, supply can become more elastic over time.

- If expanding capacity leads to higher costs (due to diminishing returns or diseconomies of scale), supply becomes less elastic.

- Different industries experience different cost increases with output.

- Primary commodity producers might face significant rises in cost due to resource constraints, making supply inelastic.

- Manufacturers with the ability to automate or enjoy economies of scale may see less steep cost increases, resulting in a more elastic supply.

### Diagram:

- A comparison of two upward-sloping supply curves: one flatter curve illustrating low cost increases (and thus more elastic supply), and one steeper curve illustrating rapid cost increases (and thus more inelastic supply).

- The flatter curve shifts more horizontally when price changes, while the steeper curve does not expand as much.

parts.content: Explain the determinants of the price elasticity of supply.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition 
- **Price Elasticity of Supply (PES):** A measure of the responsiveness of quantity supplied when there is a change in price. Calculated as the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price.

### Economic Theory

- The PES for a good is calculated through the following formula:

$$\text{PES} = \frac{\%\Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}$$

- Depending on the absolute value of the PES of a good or service, they are classified into two categories:
	- **Elastic Supply:** A situation in which a change in price leads to a proportionately greater change in quantity supplied (PES > 1).  
	- **Inelastic Supply:** A situation in which a change in price leads to a proportionately smaller change in quantity supplied (PES < 1).

- Several factors affect the PES of a good or service.

- **Time Period**  
  - In the **short run**, at least one factor of production (e.g., capital) is fixed, limiting the ability to increase output quickly. This tends to make supply more **inelastic**.  
  - In the **long run**, all factors of production are variable, giving producers more flexibility to adjust output levels. This tends to make supply more **elastic**.

- **Spare (Unused) Capacity**  
  - When firms have significant **spare capacity** (e.g., unused machinery, idle workers), it is easier to increase production if price rises, resulting in a more **elastic** supply.  
  - When firms are already operating at or near **full capacity**, increasing output quickly is challenging, resulting in a more **inelastic** supply.

- **Mobility of Factors of Production**  
  - **High mobility** (e.g., workers can be quickly retrained, machinery can be easily repurposed) allows producers to respond rapidly to price changes, increasing PES.  
  - **Low mobility** (e.g., specialized labor, equipment only usable for one type of product) restricts the ability to increase output, lowering PES.

- **Ability to Store or Stockpile the Product**  
  - If a product can be **stored** without significant cost or quality loss, firms can release or hold onto stock in response to price changes, making supply more **elastic**.  
  - If a product is **perishable** or storage is very expensive, firms cannot easily hold inventory, leading to more **inelastic** supply.

- **Length/Complexity of the Production Process**  
  - **Short/simple** production processes (e.g., basic manufacturing) allow firms to expand output relatively quickly, increasing PES.  
  - **Long/complex** production processes (e.g., sophisticated electronic devices, large-scale construction) slow down the rate at which supply can respond, reducing PES.

- **Availability and Cost of Inputs**  
  - If inputs (raw materials, labor) are **readily available** and not too costly to acquire in the needed quantity, supply can be adjusted quickly, leading to higher elasticity.  
  - If inputs are **scarce** or become significantly more expensive when trying to increase production, supply is less responsive, leading to lower elasticity.

### Diagram  
- A standard supply diagram illustrating different levels of elasticity could be used:  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e33f5c0b-30ee-4434-8325-c53de10e4f4f)

  - **Vertical (perfectly inelastic) supply curve** in the extreme short run. 
  - **Steep (relatively inelastic) supply curve** when producers have limited capacity or factor immobility.  
  - **Flatter (relatively elastic) supply curve** when producers can respond quickly due to spare capacity and factor mobility.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the effects of subsidies on all stakeholders (firms, consumers, government).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definitions 
- **Subsidy**: A payment made by the government to firms, aiming to reduce their costs of production and increase output.  
- **Stakeholders**: Groups or individuals that have an interest in, or are affected by, economic decisions (consumers, firms, government).  

### Economic Theory  
- A subsidy lowers the cost of production for firms, shifting the supply curve to the right (outward).  
- This typically leads to a decrease in market price and an increase in the equilibrium quantity.  
- Consumers benefit from lower prices and increased availability of goods. Consumer surplus generally increases as a result of the lower market price. 
- Firms receive extra revenue per unit (the difference between the price received from consumers and the per-unit subsidy from the government). Producer surplus typically increases.  
- The government bears the cost of the subsidy, which can be substantial depending on the size of the subsidy and the elasticity of supply/demand.  
- Inefficient resource allocation may arise if the subsidy distorts market signals and encourages overproduction in a sector that might otherwise be less competitive without financial support.  
- In markets with relatively elastic demand and supply, the majority of the benefit of the subsidy is likely to be passed on to consumers through lower prices. In markets with relatively inelastic supply or demand, producers may capture a larger share of the subsidy.  
- Opportunity cost occurs as government funds spent on subsidies cannot be used for other public goods or services.

---

### Diagram  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f082e6b1-4cf7-4f96-89ac-5c16b5fe0fa2)

- A standard market diagram with the supply curve shifting to the right by the amount of the subsidy.  
- Original equilibrium labeled at \(P_e, Q_e\).  
- New equilibrium labeled at \(P_{subsidized}, Q_{subsidized}\).  
- The vertical distance between the old and new supply curve represents the per-unit subsidy.  
- Highlight consumer surplus (area below demand curve and above price) and producer surplus (area above supply curve and below price) both expanding post-subsidy.  
- Show the government spending as the area between the two supply curves from the new equilibrium quantity.  

### Evaluation  
- **Short-run vs. Long-run**  
  - In the short run, consumers experience lower prices and higher consumption, while firms receive increased revenue, boosting their production.  
  - In the long run, recurring subsidy payments may burden the government budget, potentially leading to higher taxation or cuts in other areas. There is also a possibility of firms becoming dependent on subsidies and losing incentive to innovate or improve efficiency.

- **Real-world examples**  
  - In the European Union’s Common Agricultural Policy, over €50 billion was allocated to agricultural subsidies in recent years, benefiting farmers through higher incomes and stabilizing food supplies. However, critics argue that it incentivizes overproduction and strains government finances.  
  - In the United States, renewable energy producers received an estimated $18.6 billion in federal subsidies in 2021. This encouraged a shift towards cleaner energy sources but raised questions over long-term government spending and potential market distortions in the energy sector.  
  - In India, fuel and fertilizer subsidies have supported both producers and consumers, particularly in rural areas, yet government expenditure on these subsidies has been a persistent fiscal challenge, leading to concerns over budget deficits.

### Conclusion  
  - Subsidies can achieve policy goals such as improving affordability for consumers or supporting key industries.  
  - However, overreliance on subsidies can lead to government budget pressures, potential overproduction, and market inefficiencies.  
  - Appropriate subsidy levels and careful targeting may provide a balance between supporting stakeholders and ensuring long-term sustainability of government finances.

- Define key terms: inflation (a sustained increase in the general price level) and income inequality (the unequal distribution of income).  
- Explain the theoretical link: elevated inflation erodes purchasing power, disproportionately affecting lower-income groups; simultaneously, income inequality impairs consumption, reduces social mobility, and could diminish long-term growth.  
- Refer to the case study: Poland’s inflation rate surged to 14.3%, and the Gini coefficient increased to 0.32, reflecting growing inequality. The need is to alleviate the burden on lower-income households while stabilizing prices. 
- Propose policy within the syllabus: recommend a targeted fiscal policy through means-tested subsidies or direct income support to low-income households. A possible action involves reducing VAT on essential goods to mitigate price pressures, thereby addressing inflation’s immediate impact.  
- Cutting VAT on essentials lowers costs, shifting SRAS right from SRAS1 to SRAS2. This lowers the price level (pl1 -> pl2), easing cost-push inflation and increases output (Y1 -> Y2). AD stays the same, so the new equilibrium means lower inflation and higher growth, matching the policy’s aim.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/39ba1cda-faee-4b30-b884-eb6c2b3d7417)

- Highlight how the recommended policy addresses inflation: lowering indirect taxes on basic goods can reduce cost-push elements and moderate price increases; the support for low-income groups helps maintain consumption without adding excessive inflationary pressure.  
- Connect to long-term growth: narrowing income inequality may enhance human capital investment, increasing productivity. In Poland’s case, this fosters stable AD growth, reducing potential social tensions and strengthening economic performance.  
- Evaluate potential drawbacks: reduced tax revenue can strain the government budget, leading to higher public debt if not managed. However, short-term revenue loss may be offset by improved productivity and healthier consumer demand in the long run.  
- Conclude the recommendation: targeted fiscal measures that reduce living costs for lower-income households dampen the adverse effects of inflation, while also narrowing the income gap to support more sustainable economic growth.

parts.content: Explain how the mobility of factors of production affects PES in different industries.
parts.markscheme:
### Definition:

- Price Elasticity of Supply (PES): The responsiveness of the quantity supplied of a good to a change in its price.

- Factors of Production: Resources used in the production process, including land, labor, capital, and entrepreneurship.

- Factor Mobility: The ease with which factors of production can be transferred between different uses or industries.

### Economic Theory:

- PES measures how quickly and easily producers can adjust quantity supplied when price changes.

- When factor mobility is high (e.g., labor and capital can be moved quickly between different uses), supply tends to be more elastic.

- When factor mobility is low (e.g., specialized skills or capital cannot be reassigned easily), supply tends to be more inelastic.

In industries where capital is highly specialized:

- It is more difficult to switch production because machines or technology may only be suitable for one type of output.

- This makes PES relatively inelastic in the short run, as producers cannot increase supply quickly in response to higher prices.

In industries with easily transferable labor or capital:

- For example, in light manufacturing, generic machinery or widely available labor skills can shift production from one good to another with minimal time and cost.

- This higher factor mobility leads to a more elastic supply curve, as producers can respond quickly to price changes.

The time period also influences PES:

- In the short run, factor mobility is often limited because contracts, training requirements, or capital investments cannot be changed immediately.

- In the long run, factors of production can be fully adjusted, making supply more elastic if producers have enough time to re-allocate resources.


Different industries thus exhibit varying degrees of elasticity depending on how easily land, labor, and capital can be transferred to or from other uses. High mobility sustains a more elastic supply response, while low mobility restricts that response and makes supply inelastic.
### Diagram:

- A standard supply diagram can illustrate differences in PES. A steeper supply curve indicates inelastic supply (low factor mobility), while a flatter supply curve indicates elastic supply (high factor mobility).

parts.content: The government’s planned investment in high-tech industrial parks for 2023 is US$20 billion, and the MPC is estimated at 0.60. Using the Keynesian multiplier formula, calculate the total potential increase in national income resulting from this investment. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier ($k$) = $1 \div (1 - MPC) = 1 \div (1 - 0.60) = 2.5$.
• Change in National Income = $Investment \times k = US$20 \text{ billion} \times 2.5 = US$50 \text{ billion}$.
• Final answer: US$50 billion.

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for smartphones when the price increases from US$400 to US$480. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• % Change in Quantity Demanded = $[(10 - 12) \div 12] \times 100 = -16.67%$.
• % Change in Price = $[(480 - 400) \div 400] \times 100 = 20%$.
• PED = $-16.67% \div 20% = -0.833$ (or $0.833$).
• Final answer: -0.833 (or 0.833).

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for smartphones when the price increases from US$400 to US$480. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
• % Change in Quantity Supplied = $[(11 - 8) \div 8] \times 100 = 37.5%$.
• % Change in Price = $[(480 - 400) \div 400] \times 100 = 20%$.
• PES = $37.5% \div 20% = 1.875$.
• Final answer: 1.875.

parts.content: Using a demand and supply diagram for an exporting nation, explain how international trade generates a net welfare gain for a country like Vietnam in the electronics market.
parts.markscheme:
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-662c292d-bd84-4eef-b2bd-758d81d856b7-1771938518510/01534808-8027-45f2-9548-fb6b0e5de595.jpeg)
• When a country opens to trade and the world price ($P_w$) is higher than the domestic price, it will export the surplus $(Q_s - Q_d)$.
• Domestic producer surplus increases significantly as they sell more at a higher price.
• While domestic consumer surplus falls, the gain to producers exceeds the loss to consumers.
• The net welfare gain is the area of the triangle formed between the domestic demand and supply curves and the world price line.

<marks>Award 2 marks for a correctly labeled diagram showing domestic supply/demand, world price above equilibrium, and the net welfare gain triangle.</marks>
<marks>Award 2 marks for an explanation of the changes in producer/consumer surplus leading to a net gain.</marks>

• ($110 \div 370$) × 100 = 29.73%
• Final answer: 29.73%.

parts.content: Explain how international trade can allow Vietnamese firms to benefit from “economies of scale.”
parts.markscheme:
• International trade provides access to global markets, allowing Vietnamese firms to produce far larger volumes than the domestic market would support.
• Increased production levels allow firms to spread fixed costs (such as R&D or machinery) over a larger output, lowering the average cost per unit.
• Bulk-buying of raw materials and the implementation of specialized production lines become more feasible at higher outputs.
• This reduction in long-run average costs improves the international competitiveness of Vietnamese exports.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Vietnamese government could implement to move the economy from low-value assembly toward higher-value technological exports.
parts.markscheme:
• Vietnam currently relies heavily on assembly (as seen in Table 3's high Electronics revenue but dependence on imported machinery).
• A recommended policy is a supply-side investment in human capital and R&D (e.g., US$20 billion in high-tech zones mentioned in text).
• Investment in education and technical training increases labor productivity, allowing workers to perform complex innovation rather than simple assembly.
• Higher productivity shifts the Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) curve to the right, as shown in the diagram below, leading to higher potential real output and improved international competitiveness.
![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-662c292d-bd84-4eef-b2bd-758d81d856b7-1771938518510/063e21a1-1d48-458a-9175-65032293b0a4.jpeg)
• The multiplier effect of the US$20 billion investment (calculated as 2.5 in part 1) would generate a US$50 billion increase in national income, providing more tax revenue for further infrastructure.
• Moving to higher-value exports improves the Terms of Trade (Table 1 shows ToT at 93.33, suggesting imports are currently relatively expensive; higher-value exports would increase the Export Price Index).
• This reduces vulnerability to global input cost shocks and increases the value-added component of domestic production.
• Recommendation: The government should specifically target R&D subsidies and high-tech vocational training to foster local innovation and reduce the current reliance on low-margin assembly.

<marks>Award up to 4 marks for identifying and explaining a relevant policy (e.g., R&D, education) with reference to the text.</marks>
<marks>Award up to 3 marks for linking the policy to economic indicators (ToT, multiplier, value-added).</marks>
<marks>Award up to 3 marks for a clear recommendation supported by economic reasoning and diagrams.</marks>

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that Country B’s central bank could implement to control inflation while maintaining economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
Below is an example answer:

**DEFINITION**  
- Inflation: A sustained increase in the general price level. 
- Economic growth: An increase in a country's real output (real GDP) over a period time.

**MONETARY POLICY TO REDUCE INFLATION**  
- Higher interest rates reduce inflationary pressures by discouraging consumption and investment, leading to lower aggregate demand.  
- The upward trend in Country B’s interest rates (4.5% to 9.0%) has attempted to tackle the inflation surge from 3.2% to 9.3%.  
- A targeted or gradual approach to further tightening can moderate price increases without severely harming domestic demand.  

**Diagram**  
- An aggregate demand (AD) and aggregate supply (AS) diagram, where contractionary monetary policy shifts AD to the left, reducing the price level (from P to P1).  
- The reduction in output should be kept minimal if the central bank raises rates gradually, preventing a sharp decline in real GDP.  

**MAINTAINING ECONOMIC GROWTH**  
- Excessively high interest rates risk reducing investment and consumption to the point of slowing real GDP growth.  
- A measured increase in rates can stabilize price expectations, reducing the likelihood of a wage-price spiral, while leaving enough room for businesses to borrow at feasible rates.  
- Sustained investor confidence depends on predictable policy. If the central bank clarifies its inflation target and commits to moderate adjustments, firms can plan long-term capital investments without fear of abrupt borrowing cost spikes.  

**CONCLUSION**  
- A carefully calibrated, incremental rise in the interest rate is recommended to bring inflation closer to a manageable level while preserving growth prospects.  
- This policy uses the central bank’s main instrument—monetary policy—to address inflation, referencing how prior rate increases have partially contained price rises.  
- By maintaining a balance between controlling excessive price pressures and supporting business expansion, Country B can achieve lower inflation in tandem with steady economic growth.

(Other recommended policies may also be valid).

parts.content: Explain why the PES for primary commodities is generally lower than the PES for manufactured goods.
parts.markscheme:
### Definition:

- **Price Elasticity of Supply (PES)**: A measure of the responsiveness of quantity supplied of a good to a change in its price, calculated as the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price.

- **Primary Commodities**: Goods that are extracted from natural resources or grown in agriculture with minimal processing, such as agricultural products, minerals, or raw materials.

- **Manufactured Goods**: Goods that are produced through the transformation of raw materials into finished or semi-finished products, often involving multiple stages of processing and assembly.

### Economic Theory:

**Primary Commodities**

- Primary commodities often require long production periods due to biological or natural constraints (e.g., crops need time to grow, mineral extraction involves complex exploration and extraction processes). This makes them less responsive to price changes in the short run, resulting in a lower PES.

- Many primary commodities are heavily dependent on weather conditions and natural resource availability, reducing producers' ability to quickly adjust output in response to price changes. This factor further limits the elasticity of supply.

- Investment in additional capacity or infrastructure (e.g., farmland, mining equipment) to increase primary commodity output can be expensive and time-consuming. Producers may not expand or contract production quickly, which keeps PES relatively inelastic.

**Manufactured Goods**

- Manufactured goods often have more flexible production processes. Factories can adjust output levels by increasing or decreasing working shifts or by reallocating resources between different types of products, resulting in a higher PES.

- Advances in technology and capital equipment make it easier for manufacturing firms to scale production up or down quickly, increasing responsiveness to price changes. As a result, the supply of manufactured goods is more elastic.

**Conclusion**
- In some cases, manufacturers can store inputs and outputs more easily, allowing for rapid adjustments should the market price change. Primary goods (such as fresh produce) may have shorter shelf lives or require special storage conditions, limiting producers' ability to respond quickly to price changes.

- In conclusion, the typically lower (inelastic) PES of primary commodities stems from biological, time, and capacity constraints, whereas manufactured goods benefit from more flexible production methods and technologies, resulting in a higher (elastic) PES overall.

### Diagram:

- Two separate supply curves can be illustrated:
- Primary commodities represented by a steeper (more inelastic) supply curve.
- Manufactured goods represented by a flatter (more elastic) supply curve.

- The difference in steepness highlights the different degrees of responsiveness to price changes.

questions.specification:
Benin is a West African country with an estimated population of 12.5 million in 2022. Its economy is heavily reliant on agriculture, with cotton being the most important cash crop. Cotton alone accounts for roughly 25% of real GDP and over 40% of export earnings. Benin’s service sector, including transportation and trade at the Port of Cotonou, also plays a significant role in its economy.

In recent years, real GDP growth has been relatively strong, supported by public investment and higher agricultural output. Nonetheless, many people continue to live below the poverty line, and income inequality remains a concern. To address fiscal and developmental challenges, Benin’s government has been reforming its tax system, although the inefficiency of revenue collection remains an obstacle. Plans are underway to expand infrastructure at the Port of Cotonou, in the hope of further boosting economic activity.

Table 1: Macroeconomic Data for Benin (2019–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (CFA francs billions) | Real GDP Growth (%) | Inflation Rate (%) |
|------|--------------------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| 2019 | 8,000                          | 5.5                 | 1.0                |
| 2020 | 8,400                          | 5.0                 | 2.0                |
| 2021 | 9,000                          | 7.2                 | 3.3                |
| 2022 | 9,700                          | 6.5                 | 4.0                |

Table 2: Income Inequality and Population Data (Latest Estimates)

| Indicator                                               | Value          |
|---------------------------------------------------------|---------------:|
| Gini Coefficient                                        | 0.42           |
| Population below national poverty line (%)              | 38             |
| Literacy rate for population aged 15+ (%)               | 52             |
| Estimated population                                    | 12.5 million   |

Table 3: Benin’s Tax System (2022)

| Type of Tax              | Rate                             |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Corporate income tax     | 30%                              |
| Personal income tax      | Progressive, top rate at 45%     |
| VAT (standard rate)      | 18%                              |

Figure 1: Market for Cotton in Benin  
• At a price of US$0.60 per kg, quantity demanded is 3 million bales of cotton, and quantity supplied is 2.7 million bales.  
• At a price of US$0.65 per kg, quantity demanded falls to 2.8 million bales of cotton, while quantity supplied increases to 2.9 million bales.  

(Assume one “bale” is a standardized unit of cotton output.)

Furthermore, the government recently announced a US$200 million infrastructure project to expand the Port of Cotonou. Economists estimate that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Benin is 0.8, suggesting that the Keynesian multiplier effect of the project could be substantial. In addition, a recent policy discussion proposes raising VAT on luxury goods from 18% to 25%, aiming to increase tax revenues and reduce income inequality.

• Income inequality refers to an unequal distribution of income within a population; it is visible in Benin’s 0.42 Gini coefficient and 38% poverty rate.  
• Public education spending raises overall skill levels among low-income groups, addressing the 52% literacy rate and improving long-term productivity.  
• In economic theory, such spending on merit goods shifts the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve outward as the workforce becomes more skilled, raising potential output.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e4e7c3d8-877c-4cbd-92dd-24b89947f6c8)
• The data show that Benin’s strong reliance on agriculture and the service sector could benefit from better-educated workers and more equitable access to training, improving competitiveness and contributing to higher real GDP growth.  
• The multiplier effect from government spending (with an MPC of 0.8) may further stimulate real GDP and employment, especially if the spending is targeted at low-income groups with higher marginal propensities to consume.  
• The policy can reduce chronic poverty by enhancing human capital, improving future earning potential, and narrowing the income gap, leading to broader economic development.  
• Potential drawbacks include the opportunity cost of diverting funds from other sectors, but improving education is likely to offer a more sustainable long-term impact on inequality.  
• In the context of Benin’s ongoing fiscal reforms, a focus on strengthening revenue collection and using it effectively for educational investment can lower inequality and foster development.  
• Overall, this approach aligns with addressing the root causes of inequality by equipping the broader population with skills and knowledge, thereby boosting productivity and ensuring more equitable economic gains over time.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for e-service applications in Estonia as the price increases from €20 to €25 per month.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in price = ((25 − 20) / 20) × 100% = 25%  
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = ((85 − 100) / 100) × 100% = −15%  
• PED = (−15%) / (25%) = −0.6 (in absolute value, 0.6)

parts.content: Referring to Figure 1, calculate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for Estonia’s timber exports as the price changes from €100 per cubic meter to €120 per cubic meter.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in price = ((120 − 100) / 100) × 100% = 20%  
• Percentage change in quantity supplied = ((35 000 − 40 000) / 40 000) × 100% = −12.5%  
• PES = (−12.5%) / 20% = −0.625 (in absolute value, 0.625)

parts.content: Using information from the text, calculate the total Keynesian multiplier effect on real GDP if the Estonian government increases its infrastructure spending by €200 million, assuming the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.8.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − 0.8) = 5  
• Total effect on real GDP = €200 million × 5 = €1 000 million (or €1 billion)

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is a tax in which the percentage of income paid as tax rises as income increases.

parts.content: Using an aggregate demand and aggregate supply (AD/AS) diagram, explain how an additional €200 million of government spending might affect Estonia’s level of real output in the short run.

• This movement leads to an increase in real output from the initial equilibrium to a higher output level.

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, explain how progressive taxation might help reduce income inequality in Estonia.
parts.markscheme:
• Individuals earning higher incomes (above €25 000) face higher marginal tax rates, taking a larger proportion of their additional income.  
• This structure narrows after-tax income differentials because higher earners contribute a progressively larger share.  
• Lower-income groups benefit from lower tax rates, potentially increasing their disposable income proportionately more.  
• The overall effect is a reduction in income inequality, as measured by measures like the Gini coefficient.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the Estonian government could implement to support further economic growth while addressing income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Define “economic growth” as a sustained increase in a country’s real output or real GDP over time.  
• Define “income inequality” as an unequal distribution of income within a population, often measured by the Gini coefficient.  
• Consider that Estonia is a small, open economy with growing digital services and an emphasis on exports like timber, indicating a reliance on global markets.  
• Recognize that income inequality can persist despite growth; progressive taxation already exists, yet supplementary measures may be necessary to address income disparities.  
• Propose an expansion of targeted education and training programs to enhance technological and digital skills, building on Estonia’s strengths in digital services.  
• Argue that improved labor force skills lead to higher productivity and competitiveness of exports, such as e-service applications, driving economic growth.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/03a13168-fd09-47f3-8e73-31e727eb8089)
• Emphasize that equipping lower-income groups with better skills increases their potential earnings, reducing income inequality.  
• Note that the government could fund these programs through existing progressive tax revenues, ensuring that higher earners contribute a larger portion to finance inclusive growth.  
• Suggest that improved skills and higher employment would support government revenues in the long run, creating a virtuous circle of growth and reduced inequality.  
• Conclude that targeted investment in human capital is consistent with the existing digital infrastructure, addresses income imbalances, and strengthens Estonia’s position in international markets.

questions.specification:
Spain, the Eurozone’s fourth-largest economy, relies heavily on both its industrial output and its services sector, particularly tourism. After a steep downturn during the COVID-19 pandemic, Spain’s tourism industry began recovering in 2022, although higher prices have affected tourist numbers. Similarly, Spain remains the world’s largest producer of olive oil, with price fluctuations driven by changes in climate conditions, input costs, and global demand.

In 2022, the country’s real GDP was on a path to surpass pre-pandemic levels. Nonetheless, unemployment remains elevated at around 13%, especially among the youth, where it exceeds 30%. Income inequality, measured by the Gini coefficient, is moderate in European Union terms, but higher youth unemployment may exacerbate it.

Despite these challenges, the Spanish government has introduced new measures to stimulate growth, including increased infrastructure spending. Spain’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) has been estimated at 0.75 for aggregate household spending. A continued concern is whether this fiscal stimulus and other policy measures will reduce long-term unemployment and narrow income disparities.

Below are several tables containing data for the Spanish economy.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Table 1: Spanish Tourism Data (2022–2023)

| 2023 | 160                                    | 23                                    |

Gini coefficient (2022): 0.34

In 2022, the Spanish government began a new infrastructure‐spending program worth €5 billion to upgrade roads, bridges, and high‐speed rail. With an estimated marginal propensity to consume (MPC) of 0.75, policymakers hope this investment will accelerate economic growth, reduce unemployment, and limit further increases in income inequality.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Spanish tourism between 2022 and 2023.
parts.markscheme:
• % change in quantity demanded = (23 − 25) / 25 × 100% = −8%  
• % change in average daily spending (price) = (160 − 140) / 140 × 100% ≈ 14.29%  
• PED = (−8%) / (14.29%) ≈ −0.56

parts.content: Using information from the text and the MPC of 0.75, calculate the potential total increase in real GDP resulting from the €5 billion of government infrastructure spending. Assume no crowding out.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 / (1 − 0.75) = 4  
• Total increase in GDP = €5 billion × 4 = €20 billion
