parts.content: Explain why an increase in average daily tourist spending might not necessarily lead to higher total revenue for Spain’s tourism sector.
parts.markscheme:
• If demand is price elastic, a rise in price (average daily spending) could cause a proportionately larger decrease in quantity demanded, leading to lower total revenue.  
• Spain’s tourism may face competition from other destinations, so higher prices might dissuade tourists from visiting, reducing tourist numbers substantially.  
• Total revenue depends on both price and quantity; if visitor numbers fall sharply, overall tourism revenue could decline.  
• Other factors, such as global economic conditions or alternative holiday options, can influence the responsiveness of tourists to higher prices.

parts.content: Using information from the text, explain two ways in which Spain’s relatively high youth unemployment could harm its long‐term economic performance.
parts.markscheme:
• Persistent youth unemployment reduces human capital formation, as young people lose work experience and skills, lowering future productivity.  
• Higher youth unemployment can exacerbate income inequality, since prolonged joblessness limits earnings and spending power, potentially reducing long-term growth prospects.  
• If a large segment of the workforce remains unemployed, government tax revenues may fall while welfare expenditures rise, constraining resources available for investment in infrastructure and education.  
• The text indicates youth unemployment exceeds 30%, risking a cycle of lower consumption, weaker investment in skills, and reduced long-run economic competitiveness.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the Spanish government could implement to reduce youth unemployment. In your answer, justify how this policy might also help address income inequality and promote sustainable growth in the long run.
parts.markscheme:
• Structural unemployment can persist if young people lack the skills demanded by sectors such as tourism and industry; addressing it requires policies that improve skills and job matching.  
• Sustainable growth involves meeting current economic needs without harming future opportunities, suggesting that government resources should be used efficiently to develop human capital. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/63d95bbb-2cb3-4ad1-afeb-7c1e045b1b9e)
• Income inequality is measured by indices like the Gini coefficient, and with high youth unemployment at above 30%, inequality can intensify when a large cohort is left outside the labor force.  
• A targeted vocational training and apprenticeship policy could be introduced, focusing on modernizing skill sets aligned with Spain’s core sectors, including tourism, renewable energy, and advanced manufacturing.  
• Government subsidies or tax incentives for firms that hire and train young workers could reduce the immediate cost of employing unskilled youth, stimulating job creation.  
• A well-designed training program would enhance labor productivity, improve matching in the labor market, and lower structural unemployment, contributing to long-term economic performance.  
• Higher employment among youth could raise income, leading to more equitable income distribution, since low-income groups would receive opportunities to climb the income ladder.  
• Enhanced productivity and rising aggregate demand from newly employed workers could support faster real GDP growth, while a more skilled workforce would promote innovation and competitiveness over time.  
• Referring to the text, increased infrastructure spending and a 0.75 MPC suggest positive multiplier effects; linking this investment to youth training ensures human resources match improved transport and tourism facilities.  
• Although government spending on apprenticeships and training incurs an opportunity cost, the long-run benefits in reduced unemployment, improved equality, and stronger, sustainable economic growth can outweigh these costs.

questions.specification:
The island nation of Arcadia has historically been a manufacturing hub, but it is currently transitioning toward a more diversified economy with a growing technology sector. Policymakers are concerned with accurately measuring the nation's progress and the environmental impact of its industrial activities. The government is evaluating different macroeconomic indicators to better understand the standard of living and the long-term sustainability of the island's resources. 

**Table 1: Macroeconomic Data for Arcadia (2023)**

| Component | Value ($ billions) |
|-----------|--------------------|
| Household Consumption (C) | 480 |
| Gross Private Investment (I) | 120 |
| Government Expenditure (G) | 160 |
| Exports of Goods and Services (X) | 95 |
| Imports of Goods and Services (M) | 115 |
| Net Property Income from Abroad | −18 |
| Depreciation (Capital Consumption) | 45 |

In 2022, Arcadia's Real GDP was recorded at $680 billion. For the year 2023, the GDP deflator is 108 (using 2022 as the base year where the deflator was 100). The current population of Arcadia is 15 million people. 

Figure 1: The Business Cycle

![Business Cycle](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-7bbbcc47-b63a-430e-9085-dbffa50e10eb-1771938518510/c5a9a20a-f457-40d1-8464-debec08657f1.jpeg)

parts.content: Using the expenditure method and the data in Table 1, calculate the Nominal GDP for Arcadia in 2023.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{Nominal GDP} = C + I + G + (X - M)$
- $\text{Nominal GDP} = 480 + 120 + 160 + (95 - 115) = \$740 \text{ billion}$
<marks>Award 1 mark for the correct formula or working and 1 mark for the correct answer.</marks>

parts.content: Using your answer to the previous part and the provided GDP deflator, calculate Arcadia’s Real GDP for 2023.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{Real GDP} = \frac{\text{Nominal GDP}}{\text{GDP Deflator}} \times 100$
- $\text{Real GDP} = \frac{740}{108} \times 100 \approx \$685.19 \text{ billion}$
<marks>Award 1 mark for the working and 1 mark for the correct answer.</marks>

parts.content: Using the data in Table 1 and your calculated Nominal GDP, calculate Arcadia’s Gross National Income (GNI) for 2023.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{GNI} = \text{GDP} + \text{Net property income from abroad}$
- $\text{GNI} = 740 + (-18) = \$722 \text{ billion}$
<marks>Award 1 mark for the working and 1 mark for the correct answer.</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the economic growth rate for Arcadia between 2022 and 2023 based on the change in Real GDP.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{Growth Rate} = \frac{\text{Real GDP}_{2023} - \text{Real GDP}_{2022}}{\text{Real GDP}_{2022}} \times 100$
- $\text{Growth Rate} = \frac{685.19 - 680}{680} \times 100 \approx 0.76\%$
<marks>Award 1 mark for the working and 1 mark for the correct answer.</marks>

parts.content: Define the term “Purchasing Power Parity (PPP).”
parts.markscheme:
- Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) is an exchange rate adjustment that equalizes the purchasing power of different currencies by accounting for differences in the cost of living and price levels between countries.
<marks>Award 2 marks for a clear and accurate definition.</marks>

parts.content: Based on Figure 1 and your calculated growth rate for 2023, identify which point (A, B, C, or D) most likely represents Arcadia’s current economic position and explain two characteristics of that phase of the business cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- Identification: Point D (Expansion/Recovery). Although growth is low (0.76%), it is positive and follows the 2022 baseline, suggesting a movement away from a trough.
- Characteristics of Expansion: 
  1. Rising levels of Real GDP/output.
  2. Falling unemployment rates as firms hire more to meet demand.
  3. Rising consumer and business confidence.
  4. Potential for upward pressure on inflation as the output gap closes.
<marks>Award 1 mark for identification, 1 mark for justification related to the growth rate, and 2 marks for two distinct characteristics. (Total 4 marks).</marks>

parts.content: Using your calculated Nominal GDP and the population data provided, calculate the Nominal GDP per capita for Arcadia in 2023.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{GDP per capita} = \frac{\text{Total GDP}}{\text{Total Population}}$
- $\text{GDP per capita} = \frac{\$740,000,000,000}{15,000,000} \approx \$49,333.33$
<marks>Award 1 mark for the working and 1 mark for the correct answer.</marks>

parts.content: Explain two reasons why an increase in Arcadia’s Real GDP might fail to accurately reflect an improvement in the average standard of living for its citizens.
parts.markscheme:
- **Negative Externalities:** Increased production may lead to higher levels of pollution or environmental degradation, which reduces quality of life but is not subtracted from GDP figures.
- **Income Inequality:** GDP is an average measure; if the gains from growth are concentrated among a small percentage of the population, the majority may see no improvement in their living standards.
- **Non-marketed Output:** GDP does not include unpaid work (e.g., household chores, volunteering) or the shadow economy, which also contribute to welfare.
- **Composition of Output:** Growth might be driven by sectors that do not benefit consumers directly, such as increased military spending or investment in capital goods rather than consumer goods.
<marks>Award 2 marks for each well-explained reason. (Total 4 marks).</marks>

parts.content: Using the data provided and your knowledge of economics, evaluate whether the government of Arcadia should adopt “Green GDP” as its primary measure of economic success instead of Real GDP.
parts.markscheme:
- **Definition of Green GDP:** A measure of economic growth that accounts for environmental consequences by subtracting the costs of natural resource depletion and environmental degradation from standard GDP.
- **Arguments for Green GDP:** 
  - Aligns with Arcadia’s transition toward sustainability and concerns about industrial pollution.
  - Provides a more accurate reflection of "sustainable" growth by highlighting the trade-off between output and environmental health.
  - Encourages policymakers to implement 'green' policies and internalize externalities.
- **Arguments for Real GDP:**
  - Standardized measure that allows for international comparison.
  - Easier to calculate and more objective; Green GDP involves difficult and subjective valuations of environmental damage.
  - Real GDP is more useful for short-term fiscal and monetary policy planning (e.g., managing the business cycle).
- **Synthesis/Conclusion:** While Real GDP is essential for managing the macroeconomy and employment in the short run, it is insufficient for measuring long-term welfare. Arcadia should ideally use a 'dashboard' approach where Real GDP is monitored alongside Green GDP (or other indicators like HDI) to ensure that economic growth does not come at the expense of the environment.
<marks>Award up to 10 marks for a balanced evaluation that addresses both the strengths and weaknesses of the two measures and provides a justified conclusion.</marks>

questions.specification:
Italy is Europe’s third-largest economy, with significant contributions from services (including tourism), manufacturing, and agricultural exports. In 2021, tourism alone accounted for 13% of Italy’s gross domestic product (GDP). The recent global health crisis severely affected international arrivals in 2020, causing a sharp contraction in tourism revenues, before partial recovery in 2021. 

Beyond tourism, Italy is the world’s largest wine producer by volume; domestic consumption and overseas demand drive this important industry. However, wine prices and quantities can fluctuate depending on global economic conditions, exchange rates, changing tastes, and domestic supply challenges.

Between 2019 and 2021, Italy’s real GDP fell significantly in 2020 but rebounded moderately in 2021. Nonetheless, the government’s budget deficit grew, partly reflecting the necessity of higher public spending on social programs and infrastructure. Income inequality remains a concern; while Italy’s Gini coefficient stood at 0.35 in 2021, a notable proportion of the population lives close to or below the poverty line.

To tackle economic challenges and boost aggregate demand, the Italian government implemented an infrastructure-focused stimulus worth €60 billion in 2021. Economists have estimated Italy’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) at around 0.75, suggesting that fiscal stimulus measures could have a multiplying effect on national income.

Figure 1 (not to scale) represents a simplified Lorenz curve for Italy’s income distribution in 2021. Point A indicates that the bottom 40% of earners account for 20% of total income, while the top 10% account for approximately 24% of total income.

**Table 1: Macroeconomic Data for Italy (2019–2021)**

| Year | Real GDP (€ trillion) | Real GDP Growth (%) | Unemployment Rate (%) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2019 | 1.78 | 0.3  | 9.8 |
| 2020 | 1.59 | -10.7 | 11.0 |
| 2021 | 1.67 | 5.0  | 9.7 |

**Table 2: Income Distribution and Government Spending**

| Indicator                                       | Value   |
|-------------------------------------------------|---------|

questions.specification:
Lithuania is a small, open economy in Northern Europe with a population of approximately 2.8 million people. After joining the European Union in 2004, Lithuania has seen significant economic restructuring, experiencing notable growth in exports and a steady increase in real GDP over the past decade. However, challenges remain in addressing income inequality, diversifying exports, and maintaining sustainable growth.

**Table 1: Kay macroeconomic indicators of Lithuania (2021–2022)**

| Indicator                             | 2021          | 2022          |
|---------------------------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Nominal GDP (billion euros)           | 54.0          | 61.0          |
| Real GDP growth rate (%)              | 5.0           | 6.2           |
| Inflation rate (%)                    | 4.6           | 18.9          |
| Unemployment rate (%)                 | 7.1           | 6.0           |
| Corporate tax rate (%)                | 15            | 15            |
| Government expenditure (billion euros)| 13.5          | 15.0          |

According to official estimates, persistent inflationary pressures have been attributed to rising global energy prices and supply chain disruptions. Meanwhile, rapid economic growth has been driven partly by strong consumer spending and robust export performance. However, there are concerns about increasing income inequality, illustrated by the country’s Gini coefficient (reflected in Table 3).

A key export sector for Lithuania is dairy products. Lithuanian dairy firms have recently begun to export cheese and other related products to various EU and non-EU countries. Table 2 shows the changes in market data for Lithuanian cheese exports between 2021 and 2022.

**Table 2: Market Data for Lithuanian cheese exports**

|                                | 2021       | 2022       |
|--------------------------------|------------|------------|
| Average export price (€/kg)    | 4.00       | 4.40       |
| Quantity demanded (tons)       | 50 000     | 47 000     |
| Quantity supplied (tons)       | 48 000     | 50 000     |

Producers of dairy products in Lithuania face competition from other EU countries with similar climates and farming traditions. As a result, price fluctuations can affect both the quantity demanded abroad and the willingness of domestic producers to supply cheese to international markets.

Lithuania has a system of personal income tax that is partly progressive, although the corporate tax rate of 15% has remained unchanged in recent years. The government also levies indirect taxes such as value-added tax (VAT) on many consumer goods. Table 3 compares Lithuania’s Gini coefficient with that of selected countries, illustrating concerns about rising income disparity.

**Table 3: Income Distribution comparison (2021)**

| Country       | Gini Coefficient |
|---------------|------------------|
| Lithuania     | 0.36            |
| Finland       | 0.27            |
| Poland        | 0.31            |
| EU Average    | 0.30            |

Increasing government expenditure has raised questions about potential multiplier effects on the Lithuanian economy. Analysts estimate that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Lithuania stands at 0.8, which could amplify any injection of government spending in the short run.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the government of Lithuania could implement to address the challenges posed by rising inflation while also supporting long-term economic growth. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
- A suitable policy to address high inflation (18.9%) while supporting long-term growth is a targeted supply-side measure, such as government investment in renewable energy and more efficient production methods in key sectors like dairy.  
- Inflation is a persistent increase in the general price level; addressing this through improved productivity aims to lower average costs, reducing cost-push pressures and helping exports remain competitive.  
- Government investment in renewable energy and efficient production raises productivity, shifting LRAS from LRAS1 to LRAS2. This increases potential output from Y1 to Y2 and helps lower cost-push inflation without raising demand. The diagram shows how supply-side policies reduce inflationary pressure while supporting sustainable, long-term growth in Lithuania.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ba5b36f6-1ddd-433c-974b-ea4635ba2632)

- In Lithuania’s case, reducing reliance on imported energy through new infrastructure and technology could lower vulnerability to global price shocks and mitigate cost pressures highlighted by inflation data.  
- Stronger long-term growth might result from better resource allocation, encouraging foreign direct investment and sustaining the export sector’s momentum (as shown by robust export performance in the case study).  
- These policies can be complemented by continued stability in the corporate tax rate (15%) to incentivize firm expansion, while addressing income inequality through targeted government spending.  
- A lower risk of abrupt price increases supports consumer confidence, maintaining the strong consumer spending observed in Lithuania and preventing overheating of the economy.  
- A balanced focus on productivity ensures that limited government funds are put toward projects with a high multiplier effect, leveraging the country’s MPC of 0.8 to stimulate economic activity with less inflationary pressure.  
- Reducing inflationary pressures preserves purchasing power, boosting domestic welfare and sustaining demand for local goods.  
- Encouraging innovation and improved efficiency in production drives economic diversification, supports long-term growth, and aligns with lower inflation targets.

questions.specification:
Nigeria, located in West Africa, has Africa’s largest population of over 220 million people. Despite significant natural resource wealth, especially in oil and natural gas, a large proportion of the population lives below the international poverty line. In recent years, diversification into agriculture and manufacturing has gained momentum. However, infrastructure shortfalls and income inequality remain major challenges.  

Agriculture accounts for about 23% of Nigeria’s GDP, with cocoa among its leading non‐oil exports. The country’s heavy reliance on crude oil as a major source of export revenue makes it vulnerable to volatile global commodity prices. Meanwhile, an increasing share of government budget is allocated to capital projects—such as roads, electricity grids, and telecommunications—to stimulate long‐term growth.

Despite these initiatives, the government faces pressure to generate higher tax revenue and improve the standard of living. The highest personal income tax rate is 24%, while corporate taxation is set at 30%. Nigeria’s value‐added tax (VAT) rate was recently raised from 5% to 7.5%, although many essential food products remain VAT‐exempt in an effort to protect low‐income households.

Table 1 provides selected macroeconomic and labor market indicators for Nigeria:


**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic and Labour Market Indicators for Nigeria**

| Year | Real GDP (billion US$) | Population (million) | Unemployed (million) | Employed (million) |
|-----:|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 2019 | 420                    | 200                  | 18                   | 70                 |
| 2020 | 400                    | 205                  | 23                   | 72                 |
| 2021 | 410                    | 210                  | 25                   | 76                 |
| 2022 | 450                    | 215                  | 26                   | 80                 |


Despite a slight slowdown in 2020, the economy rebounded by 2022 with growing output in both the oil and non‐oil sectors. However, unemployment rates and underemployment remain high, reflecting rapid population growth and limited formal job opportunities.

Nigeria’s cocoa output has also increased, largely due to policy support and higher global cocoa prices. Table 2 shows how domestic demand for cocoa beans in Nigeria changed when the global price rose from US\$2,000 per tonne to US\$2,300 per tonne between 2021 and 2022:


**Table 2: Cocoa Market Data in Nigeria**

| Price of Cocoa (US$/tonne) | Quantity Demanded Domestically (million tonnes/year) |
|----------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| 2,000                      | 1.2                                                |
| 2,300                      | 1.0                                                |


Meanwhile, Nigeria’s government is committed to infrastructure spending to spur economic development. The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Nigeria is estimated at 0.8, though this may vary across income groups. Policymakers hope that infrastructure projects financed partly by new corporate taxes will reduce production costs for businesses, boost employment, and narrow Nigeria’s Gini coefficient of 0.38.

- Unemployment refers to people of working age who are without work, available for work, and actively seeking employment.  
- Economic development can be described as a sustained increase in living standards that involves improving education, health, and per capita income, especially in the context of reducing poverty and inequality.

- A government-led program to subsidize or offer targeted investment incentives for non-oil sectors, such as agriculture and agro-processing, can reduce over-reliance on volatile oil revenues.  
- Agriculture employs more than 35% of the Nigerian labor force and shows growth potential in cocoa production, suggesting capacity to absorb unemployed workers.  
- Investing in infrastructure (transport, storage, and processing facilities) would reduce production costs, boost productivity, and enhance competitiveness in global markets.  
- Cutting taxes or providing loan guarantees for agricultural processing firms encourages capital investment, raises output, and generates new jobs.  
- Rising global cocoa prices shown by the shift from US\$2,400 to US$2,700 per metric ton can motivate firms to scale up production if supportive policies exist.  
- Such policies influence aggregate supply by lowering costs of production and increase employment, leading to higher real output and incomes.  
- Although subsidies and tax incentives may reduce short-term government revenue, they can broaden the tax base over time as a more diversified economy expands overall tax receipts.  
- Reducing unemployment improves long-term growth prospects and reduces inequality, aligning with the broader development goals mentioned in the text.  
- These measures can help stabilize the country’s export earnings, mitigating the risks associated with volatile oil revenue and fostering inclusive economic development.

questions.specification:
**Text A: The Coffee Market in Country Z**

Country Z is a major producer of coffee beans, contributing significantly to its export revenues. However, due to a global oversupply of coffee, prices have fallen dramatically, impacting the incomes of local farmers. To support farmers, the government introduced a price floor above the equilibrium price. While this has stabilized farmer incomes, it has resulted in surplus production and increased government spending on storing unsold coffee.

In response to environmental concerns, Country Z has also introduced subsidies for farmers who adopt sustainable farming methods. Critics argue that while these measures aim to support farmers, they distort market efficiency and divert resources from other sectors of the economy.

parts.content: Using a market failure diagram, explain how the subsidy for sustainable farming in Country Z may lead to inefficiencies.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d90451a1-b906-4287-95a7-eab9abf90270)

The diagram shows a subsidy for sustainable farming in Country Z's coffee market.
- Initially, equilibrium is at Pe and Qe (where S and D intersect).
- Government subsidy shifts the supply curve to the right (S-sub).

After the subsidy:
- Consumer price falls from Pe to Pc. Consumer surplus increases (gains pink area).
- Producers receive price Pp (equals Pc + subsidy). Producer surplus increases (gains blue area).
- Quantity increases from Qe to Qsub.
- Government spends (Pp-Pc)xQsub.
- There is welfare loss equal to Government spending minus gains in surplus (WL). This arises due to overproduction.
- Hence, since there is welfare loss, the introduction of the subsidies has created inefficiencies in the market.

<marks>2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks>4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of agricultural subsidies on economic growth and/or development in Country Y.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### **Definition**  
- **Agricultural subsidies**: Financial support provided by the government to farmers to lower production costs, stabilize incomes, and enhance productivity.  
- **Economic growth**: An increase in a country's real output (real GDP) over a period time. 
- **Economic development**: The process of increasing real per capita income while improving living standards and reducing poverty within an economy as whole.  

- Economic growth refers to increases in real output over time, whereas economic development refers to a process that leads to improved living standards for a population as a whole. 
- Increasing levels of output and incomes resulting from economic growth do not, by itself, guarantee economic development.

### **Impact on Economic Growth**  
#### **Increased Agricultural Productivity and Output**  
- The subsidies enable **small-scale farmers to invest in better technology, fertilizers, and irrigation systems**, leading to **higher agricultural productivity**.  
- Increased productivity can raise **total agricultural output**, contributing to **higher GDP growth**.  
- The **trade bloc allows tariff-free exports**, providing an **expanded market for agricultural goods**, potentially boosting **export revenue**.  
- The **GDP growth rate has risen from 2.8% (2019) to 4.2% (2022)**, indicating that economic growth has strengthened alongside the implementation of subsidies (Table 2).  

#### **Limitations**  
- Despite rising exports, **imports have increased at a faster rate**, leading to a **persistent trade deficit (-3.8 billion USD in 2022)** (Table 2).  
- If subsidies **reduce price competitiveness**, farmers may become reliant on government support instead of improving efficiency.  
- Agricultural subsidies **divert government funds** that could be used to support industrial development, slowing long-term economic diversification.  

### **Impact on Economic Development**  
#### **Improved Rural Incomes and Poverty Reduction**  
- Small-scale farmers, who are often part of the **lower-income population**, receive direct financial support, leading to **higher disposable incomes**.  
- Improved income levels can lead to **better access to healthcare, education, and nutrition**, promoting **long-term human capital development**.  
- Higher rural incomes can boost **aggregate demand**, stimulating **consumption-led growth** in other sectors.  

#### **Limitations**  
- If subsidies are **not well-targeted**, larger and wealthier landowners may benefit more than small-scale farmers, leading to **income inequality**.  
- Subsidies **do not guarantee structural transformation**, and reliance on **low-value-added agriculture** limits **long-term economic mobility**.  
- The focus on agriculture may prevent the **reallocation of labor to higher-value industries**, slowing the transition to a more industrialized economy.  

### **Sustainability of Agricultural Subsidies**  
#### **Short-Term vs. Long-Term Impacts**  
- In the **short term**, subsidies support economic development by **improving incomes and food security**.  
- In the **long term**, continued reliance on subsidies may lead to **inefficiencies**, as **farmers lack incentives to innovate** or adopt market-driven practices.  
- The **opportunity cost of subsidies** is significant, as funds could be allocated to **infrastructure, education, or industrial investment**, sectors that drive **sustained economic development**.  

### **Overall Effectiveness in Promoting Economic Growth and Development**  
#### **Strengths**  
- **Increased agricultural productivity** enhances **food security** and **export potential**, supporting GDP growth.  
- **Improved rural incomes** contribute to **higher standards of living**, fostering economic development.  
- The **removal of tariffs within the trade bloc** allows for greater **market access**, increasing trade potential.  

#### **Weaknesses**  
- **Trade deficits persist**, indicating **structural weaknesses in the economy**, with reliance on **imported goods** despite rising exports.  
- **Limited industrialization** due to subsidies prioritizing agriculture over high-value sectors, slowing **long-term economic diversification**.  
- **Fiscal strain on government finances**, as subsidies require significant expenditure, potentially reducing investments in **infrastructure, healthcare, and education**.  

#### **Conclusion**  
Agricultural subsidies in Country Y contribute to **short-term economic growth and development** by increasing **rural incomes, productivity, and food security**. However, their long-term sustainability is questionable, as they **divert resources from industrial development**, potentially **delaying economic diversification and structural transformation**. To enhance effectiveness, policies should focus on **gradually reducing subsidies while investing in productivity-enhancing infrastructure and industrial expansion** to create a **more resilient and diversified economy**.

questions.specification:
Malaysia is a rapidly developing Southeast Asian nation with a population of about 32 million people. Over the past decade, the country has experienced steady economic expansion, supported by both the manufacturing and services sectors. However, agriculture remains a significant contributor to export revenues, particularly from palm oil. The government has implemented a range of macroeconomic policies to encourage growth, reduce unemployment, and address income inequality.

According to World Bank estimates, Malaysia’s real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) reached US$338.3 billion in 2021 and increased to US$370.1 billion in 2022 (see Table 1). Rising global demand for palm oil led to higher prices in 2022, boosting export revenue but also creating concerns about over-reliance on a single primary commodity. At the same time, Malaysia’s unemployment rate has fallen from 4.6% to 3.8%, partly due to government-sponsored job placement programs.

The government levies both indirect (sales and service tax of 6%) and direct taxes. Corporate income tax stands at 24%, although there are plans to reduce it to 22% to stimulate greater foreign direct investment (FDI). Progressive personal income taxes range from 1% up to 28%, depending on income brackets (see Table 3).

Recently, the government announced a stimulus package of MYR 20 billion (approximately US$4.5 billion) intended to boost aggregate demand. The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) among Malaysian households is estimated at 0.75, implying that the stimulus may have a significant multiplier effect on national income. Nonetheless, policymakers remain concerned about persistent income inequality, as measured by the Gini coefficient (0.41 in 2021 and 0.40 in 2022), and are exploring additional redistributive measures.

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Malaysia (2021–2022)

| Indicator                     | 2021    | 2022    |
|-------------------------------|---------|---------|
| Real GDP (billion US$)       | 338.3   | 370.1   |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)     | 3.1     | 8.7     |
| Inflation Rate (%)           | 2.5     | 3.3     |
| Unemployment Rate (%)        | 4.6     | 3.8     |

Table 2: Malaysian Palm Oil Data (2021 vs. 2022)

| Year | Price (US$/metric tonne) | Quantity Demanded (million tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (million tonnes) |
|------|--------------------------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| 2021 | 620                      | 2.10                               | 1.80                               |
| 2022 | 750                      | 2.00                               | 1.95                               |

Table 3: Income Distribution and Taxation in Malaysia (2022)

| Income Bracket (MYR/year) | Marginal Tax Rate (%) |
|---------------------------|------------------------|
| 0 – 35,000                | 1                      |
| 35,001 – 50,000           | 8                      |
| 50,001 – 70,000           | 14                     |
| 70,001 – 100,000          | 21                     |
| 100,001 – 250,000         | 24                     |
| 250,001 – 400,000         | 28                     |

The government’s intention is to diversify the Malaysian economy further—reducing the reliance on palm oil exports—and to address growing concerns about income inequality through progressive taxation and social welfare programs. Policymakers believe that careful use of fiscal policy (including stimulus measures and adjustments to corporate tax rates) and structural reforms can support sustainable long-term growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Malaysian government to reduce income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• “Income inequality” and “progressive taxation” represent key terms. Income inequality refers to the uneven distribution of income across the population. A progressive tax system is one in which the tax rate increases as income rises.  
• Higher progressive taxation on upper brackets can generate more government revenue, which can be redistributed through public spending and welfare programs. This can address persistent inequality suggested by the Gini coefficient (0.41 in 2021 to 0.40 in 2022).  
• Increasing tax rates specifically in higher income brackets or closing loopholes ensures that those with greater ability to pay contribute more, while minimizing the burden on lower incomes. Reference to Table 3 shows that the current highest rate is 28%. Adjusting this rate could intensify redistributive effects.  
• Government expenditure funded by additional tax revenue could target essential services such as education, healthcare, and skills training, promoting equal opportunities and reducing the reliance on palm oil for employment. This supports the diversification strategy highlighted in the case study.  
• Allocating part of the increased revenue to direct transfer payments for lower-income individuals could reduce immediate financial difficulties and increase aggregate demand, consistent with the multiplier effect described (MPC of 0.75).  
• In the long run, educating and training the workforce can sustain productivity growth, expanding potential output and reducing the risk of over-reliance on commodities. This aligns with the government’s plan for sustainable development and structural reforms.  
• This approach would tackle income inequality by directly redistributing income, helping lower-income groups while maintaining economic stability. An increase in their disposable income also supports domestic industries and encourages growth, further consolidating the long-term external stability sought by policymakers.  
• The policy integrates well with current fiscal measures and works alongside programs that promote job placement, potentially lowering unemployment and supporting broader-based growth.  
• Reducing inequality in this manner could help maintain social cohesion, ensuring that the benefits of economic expansion are shared more evenly.  
• The recommended policy uses progressive taxation as a direct tool to achieve equity, which is consistent with Malaysia’s emphasis on reducing the Gini coefficient and developing a more resilient economy.

questions.specification:
Ghana is one of the leading cocoa producers in the world, second only to Côte d’Ivoire. Cocoa has long served as a major source of export revenue, accounting for up to 20% of total merchandise exports. In addition to cocoa, Ghana depends on mineral exports, especially gold. The services sector has expanded in recent years, contributing significantly to Ghana’s GDP.

Between 2021 and 2022, Ghana’s real GDP grew by an estimated 4.2%. However, rising global commodity prices have increased Ghana’s inflation rate to over 30% in 2023, a situation complicated by higher costs of imported fuel and raw materials. The country has also faced a widening fiscal deficit due to increased government expenditure and relatively low tax revenue collection.

In an effort to diversify Ghana’s exports, the government has introduced incentives for local entrepreneurs, including lower corporate income taxes for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). Still, many economists argue that structural challenges, such as income inequality and limited infrastructure, could hinder long-term economic development. The Gini coefficient for Ghana stands at approximately 0.43, reflecting moderate income inequality. 

The Ghanaian government has been exploring both demand-side and supply-side policies to spur more robust and inclusive growth. On the demand side, it has increased spending on infrastructure (roads and energy), while on the supply side, it has encouraged private investment in processing facilities for cocoa and other agricultural products, hoping to move up the value chain.

TABLE 1: DOMESTIC DEMAND AND SUPPLY DATA FOR COCOA IN GHANA

| Cocoa Price (US$/tonne) | Quantity Demanded (thousand tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (thousand tonnes) |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 2200                    | 710                                  | 530                                  |
| 2500                    | 680                                  | 560                                  |

Notes:  
• In 2022, Ghana exported 560 thousand tonnes of cocoa out of its total production.  
• World market prices for cocoa increased from US$2200 per tonne to US$2500 per tonne over the last harvest season.  

TABLE 2: SELECTED MACROECONOMIC INDICATORS FOR GHANA

| Indicator                              | 2021    | 2022    | 2023 (est.) |
|----------------------------------------|---------|---------|-------------|
| Real GDP (US$ billions)               | 67      | 70      | 73          |
| Annual Real GDP Growth Rate (%)        | 3.5     | 4.2     | 4.0         |
| Tax Revenue (GH₵ billions)            | 55.0    | 58.8    | 64.0        |
| Government Expenditure (GH₵ billions) | 70.0    | 76.2    | 85.0        |
| Inflation Rate (%)                     | 12.5    | 25.0    | 31.5        |
| Gini Coefficient                       | 0.43    | 0.43    | 0.43        |
| Unemployment Rate (%)                  | 11.2    | 11.5    | 12.0        |

Notes:  
• GH₵ refers to Ghanaian cedi (national currency).

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the government of Ghana in order to address the country’s rising inflation rate. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Inflation is the sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over time.  
• Monetary policy involves the central bank adjusting interest rates and the money supply to influence overall economic activity, including the rate of inflation.  
• Ghana’s inflation has climbed to over 30%, partly due to higher global commodity prices and imported fuel costs, suggesting both cost-push and demand-pull elements.  
• Tightening monetary policy by raising interest rates could help reduce aggregate demand and stabilize prices by discouraging excessive consumer and business borrowing. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ca3cdbb3-98a9-4be0-b8b4-b7619ec3336b)
• Higher interest rates may slow down real GDP growth initially, but they can help anchor inflation expectations and currency stability, especially given rising import costs.  
• Contractionary monetary policy could reduce imported inflation by making the Ghanaian cedi relatively stronger if higher interest rates attract foreign capital.  
• Table 2 shows notable increases in government expenditure, which can add demand-pull pressures; complementary fiscal restraint (reducing some government spending) could reinforce the effect of tighter monetary policy.  
• A coordinated approach that combines moderate interest rate hikes with targeted government spending cuts in non-priority projects could address Ghana’s fiscal deficit while stabilizing the price level.  
• Structural issues such as income inequality (Gini coefficient of 0.43) and limited infrastructure remain challenges to sustainable growth, but containing inflation is crucial for long-term investment and confidence.  
• Implementing a carefully paced contractionary monetary policy, complemented by prudent fiscal measures, is recommended to lower inflation, maintain investor confidence, and support a more stable macroeconomic environment over time.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of Country X could implement to reduce its trade deficit while sustaining economic growth. Justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
Below is an example answer:

**DEFINITION**  
- Trade deficit: Occurs when the value of a country’s imports exceeds the value of its exports, resulting in a negative trade balance.  
- Economic growth: An increase in a country’s real output (real GDP) over time a period of time.

**LINKING THE TRADE DEFICIT AND ECONOMIC GROWTH**  
- Data in Table 4 shows a persistent negative trade balance for Country X, though the deficit narrowed from -20 to -10 billion dollars between 2020 and 2023.  
- Sustaining real GDP growth (Table 2) remains a priority, given that real GDP expansion has accelerated from 3.8% in 2020 to 5.3% in 2023.  
- A policy that addresses the deficit while maintaining growth should reduce reliance on imports, encourage domestic production, and protect rising incomes.  
- Higher household income, reflected in Table 2 (increasing from $25,000 to $28,879), indicates stronger purchasing power, which fuels import demand. Without intervention, this can worsen the trade deficit if domestic substitutes are not available.  

**TARIFF AS A POTENTIAL POLICY MEASURE**  
- Imposing tariffs on selected imports (for example, products that compete directly with domestically produced goods) can shift consumer demand toward local producers, helping to reduce import expenditure.  
- Domestic industries that have the capacity to meet local demand may expand output, contributing to real GDP growth.  
- Luxury products with high YED (Table 1: YED = 2.0) face strong demand as incomes rise; tariffs on comparable imported luxury items could curtail import volumes while allowing domestic luxury producers to flourish.  

**Diagram**  
- A standard tariff diagram with domestic supply (S), world price (Pw), and the new price (Pw + tariff).  
- The rise in price due to the tariff reduces import quantity and encourages domestic production, shown by an outward extension of the domestic supply curve.  
- Consumer surplus falls, but domestic producer surplus increases, contributing to employment and potentially to further investment in local industries.  

**EVALUATION AND JUSTIFICATION**  
- A tariff can generate government revenue and lessen the trade deficit by discouraging imports; part of the revenue may be used to support strategic industries or invest in public infrastructure.  
- Excessive tariffs risk higher inflationary pressures (already at 3.5% according to Table 2), which could erode household purchasing power and slow economic growth.  
- Retaliation from trade partners may reduce exports; however, if the focus remains on products where domestic producers are already competitive, the negative impact may be minimized.  
- This policy aligns with the goal of narrowing the trade deficit from its current level (Table 4) and simultaneously supports continued real GDP growth, leveraging the existing upward trend in domestic demand and production.  

**CONCLUSION**  
- A carefully targeted tariff on specific imports is recommended to reduce the trade deficit and sustain economic growth.  
- Revenue from the tariff can be channeled into further economic development, mitigating possible inflationary pressures through strategic investment in domestic industries and infrastructure.  
- This approach can balance external accounts while preserving Country X’s positive growth trajectory, as seen in the data on rising GDP and household incomes.

(Other recommended policies may also be valid).

Ethiopia, located in the Horn of Africa, has recently been one of the fastest-growing economies in sub-Saharan Africa. Agriculture accounts for 76% of total employment and around 32% of GDP. Coffee is Ethiopia’s primary export, contributing nearly 30% of the country’s export revenues. The government has been investing heavily in infrastructure—particularly in constructing industrial parks—to encourage more manufacturing activity, diversify the economy, and reduce its dependence on agriculture. 

Despite this growth, Ethiopia continues to experience challenges related to income inequality, as indicated by a Gini coefficient of 0.38. Tax revenue collection remains low, and the government’s ability to finance essential public services is limited. In 2022, the Ethiopian government introduced an expansionary fiscal policy, allocating US$2.5 billion (around 25% more than the previous year) towards rural infrastructure. Policymakers hope this increase in government spending will boost aggregate demand, stimulate growth in the manufacturing sector, and create new jobs. 

In March 2022, an Ethiopian manufacturing firm purchased new machinery at a total cost of 575 000 Ethiopian birr (ETB), inclusive of the standard 15% VAT rate. The firm aims to expand its processing capacity for coffee beans destined for foreign markets.

• Definition: Industrial policy involves government action aimed at developing specific sectors that can drive growth and structural transformation.

• Allocating part of the US$2.5 billion infrastructure budget to building technology hubs, improving transport networks, and providing business support services can lower production costs and attract investments.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about a producer's responsiveness to price changes, which is the definition of Price Elasticity of Supply (PES). The ability to store goods (inventories) is one of the primary determinants of PES.

parts.content: Explain how the ability to store goods affects a producer's responsiveness to price changes.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition:

- **Price Elasticity of Supply (PES)**: A measure of the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in the price of a good, ceteris paribus.

- **Storable Goods**: Goods that can be held in inventory or warehouses for later sale.

- **Responsiveness**: The degree to which producers can adjust output in reaction to price fluctuations.

### Economic Theory:

- Producers who can store goods are able to withhold or release stock in response to price changes. When market prices rise, these producers can quickly increase supply by selling goods from storage, making supply more price elastic.

- Conversely, when prices fall, producers can restrict supply by holding more goods in storage to wait for better prices. This again contributes to a more elastic supply curve because quantity supplied changes more significantly relative to the price change.

- If a good is not easily storable (e.g., perishable items), producers have limited ability to respond to price changes in the short run, resulting in a more inelastic supply curve.

- Storage capacity increases producers' flexibility and reduces the time needed to adjust output levels. Over shorter periods, producers with inventories can change their output decisions more readily (e.g., releasing or withholding stock), contrasting with those who must produce anew.

- The cost of storage can influence how much inventory is kept. If storage costs are low, producers have a greater incentive to store goods and adjust supply in response to price changes, contributing to higher PES. If storage costs are high, the benefit of holding inventories diminishes, reducing the elasticity of supply.

- Over longer periods, even goods that are difficult to store benefit from technological improvements or increased storage investment, allowing producers to gradually enhance their responsiveness to price changes.

### Diagram:

**Supply curves with varying slopes**

- A more elastic supply curve for goods that can be stored (flatter slope), reflecting a higher responsiveness to price changes due to stored inventory.

- A more inelastic supply curve for goods that cannot be stored (steeper slope), showing limited responsiveness in the short run.

Greece, located in Southeast Europe, has traditionally relied on tourism, shipping, and agriculture as key sectors of its economy. Following the global financial crisis, the Greek government underwent several reforms aimed at stabilizing its public finances and boosting economic growth. Although the economy shows signs of recovery, it still faces challenges in terms of high unemployment, tax revenue collection, and income inequality.

The tourism sector remains a driving force. In 2019, tourism accounted for almost 20% of Greece’s GDP. However, demand for hotel stays is sensitive to price changes. Agriculture also plays a significant role, with olive oil being one of Greece’s major exports.

In recent years, Greece has pursued measures to tackle tax evasion and to improve government finances. Indirect taxes (such as VAT) on many goods and services are relatively high compared to other EU countries. The government has also considered expansionary fiscal policy, hoping to capitalize on a positive Keynesian multiplier effect to stimulate growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the Greek government could implement to support long-term economic growth while addressing income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Definition of key terms: “economic growth” is the increase in real output over time; “income inequality” refers to an unequal distribution of income within a population; “fiscal policy” indicates government use of taxation and spending to influence the economy.  
• Explanation/Economic Theory:  
  – A supply-side approach, such as government investment in education and training, can enhance labor productivity and raise the long-term productive capacity (LRAS).
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b6e676c3-2c8a-404d-9ab5-fb75a7282e74)
  – Improved human capital attracts more investment, especially in key Greek sectors like shipping, tourism, and agriculture, boosting potential growth.  
  – Higher-skilled workers earn higher wages, reducing income inequality when compared to less-skilled labor.  
  – By referencing the case study’s challenges (high unemployment and income inequality), allocating funds toward targeted retraining programs addresses structural unemployment and improves equity.  
  – The policy’s effectiveness relies on sufficient budget allocations without causing unsustainable debt, aligning with previous expansionary fiscal efforts highlighted in the text.  
• Evaluation/Synthesis with Data:  
  – Increased government spending on education and skill development could capitalize on the positive Keynesian multiplier shown by the case study, stimulating GDP growth in the short run.  
  – In the long run, better-skilled labor may raise productivity in core sectors like agriculture (olive oil exports) and tourism, enhancing competitiveness.  
  – A targeted approach can mitigate the distributional effects of high indirect taxation (24% VAT), ensuring lower-income groups receive more direct benefits.  
  – An opportunity cost exists because funds used for education and training could be diverted from other areas in government spending.  
  – Progress may be gradual, but focusing on structural improvements could help sustain broad-based growth and narrow the Gini coefficient over time.  
• Concluding Recommendation:  
  – Allocating resources toward education and training programs supports long-term growth by increasing the economy’s productive capacity.  
  – The policy promotes inclusive growth and reduces income inequality by equipping the labor force with skills that match industry demands.  
  – This course of action addresses both economic expansion and equity issues observed in the Greek context, contributing to a more balanced and sustainable recovery.

• Income inequality refers to how income is distributed among individuals or households in an economy, often measured by the Gini coefficient.  
• A progressive tax system channels greater contributions from higher-income earners, aiding equity and funding public welfare, aligning with Finland’s current system shown in Table 3.

• The short-run stimulus can arise as government spending in innovation and training raises aggregate demand, creating jobs and encouraging consumption, consistent with the Keynesian multiplier effect mentioned in the text.  
• Maintaining a progressive tax rate ensures that the benefits of higher growth do not compromise low income inequality, aligning with the data showing Finland’s relatively low Gini coefficient of 0.27.  
• Stable income distribution encourages broad-based consumption, increasing aggregate demand and reinforcing the growth triggered by innovation spending, which helps balance equity and growth objectives.  
• Potential drawbacks include higher government expenditure generating budget pressures, but Finland’s comprehensive welfare system and prudent fiscal history suggest that targeted R&D investments are feasible.  
• This policy aims to improve Finland’s competitiveness in global markets, support output growth, and preserve its low Gini coefficient through continued progressive taxation and inclusive welfare structures.

parts.content: Using Figure 1, the government spending rises by US\$1 billion, yet real GDP rises by US\$2.5 billion. Calculate the government spending multiplier and explain the main step used in your calculation.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of Guatemala could implement to reduce income inequality. Justify the recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
- Income inequality, shown by a Gini coefficient of about 0.48 and the top 20% receiving 47.9% of income, indicates a need for stronger redistribution.  
- A policy that increases the progressivity of direct taxes (such as raising the top personal income tax rate from its current 31%) can collect additional revenue from higher-income groups to reduce the burden on lower-income households.  
- Progressive taxes reduce the share of disposable income at higher levels while minimizing the impact on low-income earners, supporting equity and potentially lowering the reliance on regressive taxes like the 12% VAT that applies to most goods.  
- Additional revenues from higher direct taxes can be directed into enhanced social programs and infrastructure (as indicated by existing government spending on these areas), which can help expand educational opportunities, healthcare, and rural development.  
- Stronger progressive taxation increases revenue for public investment in education and infrastructure, improving human capital and productive capacity. This causes the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) to shift right from LRAS1 to LRAS2, raising real output from Y1 to Y2. The diagram shows how reducing income inequality through structural investment supports sustainable economic growth in Guatemala.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/36a16a03-5dcf-4910-80ba-be7c8bf3f347)

- The injection of more resources into rural areas can stimulate economic activity, raise living standards for lower-income households, and improve long-term productivity.  
- Higher government expenditure on education aligns with the agricultural nature of Guatemala’s economy and promotes skill development, addressing rural legacies of poverty and supporting growth in sectors like coffee production.  
- The policy can work effectively with existing measures, as Guatemala already employs progressive personal income taxes, but stronger enforcement and possibly higher top rates can ensure a more balanced distribution of income.  
- The proposal can face political constraints or potential capital flight, requiring careful planning to maintain foreign direct investment and macroeconomic stability.  
- The overall aim is to improve the income share of lower quintiles while encouraging long-term gains in productivity and social cohesion, reducing the chronic inequality that hinders development.  
- In conclusion, strengthening progressive taxation and utilizing the additional revenue for social and infrastructural development in poorer regions can reduce inequality and support sustainable economic growth.

- Job training programs improve the quality and productivity of the labor force (human capital).
- This enhances the economy's productive capacity, shifting the Production Possibility Frontier (PPF) outward (from PPF1 to PPF2).
- This shift represents potential economic growth, as the economy is now capable of producing more goods and services than before.


![PPF diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-d915067b-d9dc-4b5a-bd5a-5f9954bdd457-1771938518510/99829b2e-0f9c-498e-9875-a5653c262dec.jpeg)

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the effectiveness of using supply-side policies versus demand-management policies to ensure long-term price stability and growth in Arcadia during its green transition.
parts.markscheme:
- Demand-management policies (e.g., interest rate hikes) can effectively control short-run inflation by reducing AD, as shown in the CPI increase to 108.2. However, they may risk slowing real GDP growth (which was only 1.9% from 2022-2023).
- Supply-side policies, such as the $50 billion green infrastructure initiative and labor training, address the root of the transition by shifting the Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) to the right.
- A rightward shift in LRAS (from LRAS1 to LRAS2) allows for an increase in potential output (Yf1 to Yf2) without causing inflationary pressure, which is crucial given the CPI rise. 

![LRAS shift](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-d915067b-d9dc-4b5a-bd5a-5f9954bdd457-1771938518510/0dd20ed1-bd74-43b8-a1cd-34ac58a7935e.jpeg)

- Supply-side measures improve efficiency and can reduce structural unemployment (implied by the training programs), whereas interest rate hikes primarily target cyclical issues.
- However, supply-side policies have long time lags and high opportunity costs ($50 billion spending), while interest rates can be adjusted quickly by the central bank.
- In the case of Arcadia, a combination is likely best: monetary policy to stabilize the CPI in the short run, and supply-side investment to ensure the green transition leads to sustainable long-term growth.

Spain, the Eurozone’s fourth-largest economy, relies heavily on both its industrial output and its services sector, particularly tourism. After a steep downturn during the COVID-19 pandemic, Spain’s tourism industry began recovering in 2022, although higher prices have affected tourist numbers. Income inequality, measured by the Gini coefficient, is moderate in European Union terms, but higher youth unemployment may exacerbate it.

Despite these challenges, the Spanish government has introduced new measures to stimulate growth, including increased infrastructure spending. Spain’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) has been estimated at 0.75 for aggregate household spending. A continued concern is whether this fiscal stimulus and other policy measures will reduce long-term unemployment and narrow income disparities.

parts.markscheme:

- Sustainable growth involves meeting current economic needs without harming future opportunities, suggesting that government resources should be used efficiently to develop human capital.  
- Income inequality is measured by indices like the Gini coefficient, and with high youth unemployment at above 30%, inequality can intensify when a large cohort is left outside the labor force.  
- A targeted vocational training and apprenticeship policy could be introduced, focusing on modernizing skill sets aligned with Spain’s core sectors, including tourism, renewable energy, and advanced manufacturing.  
- Vocational training boosts human capital and productivity, shifting LRAS from LRAS1 to LRAS2. Real output increases from Y1 to Y2, showing sustainable, long-term growth. - A well-designed training program would enhance labor productivity, improve matching in the labor market, and lower structural unemployment, contributing to long-term economic performance.  
- Higher employment among youth could raise income, leading to more equitable income distribution, since low-income groups would receive opportunities to climb the income ladder.  
- Enhanced productivity and rising aggregate demand from newly employed workers could support faster real GDP growth, while a more skilled workforce would promote innovation and competitiveness over time.  
- Referring to the text, increased infrastructure spending and a 0.75 MPC suggest positive multiplier effects; linking this investment to youth training ensures human resources match improved transport and tourism facilities.  
- Although government spending on apprenticeships and training incurs an opportunity cost, the long-run benefits in reduced unemployment, improved equality, and stronger, sustainable economic growth can outweigh these costs.

Italy is among the world’s leading exporters of wine, with key markets in the United States, Germany, and the United Kingdom. Domestic consumption of wine has remained stable but is sensitive to fluctuations in price and incomes, especially in regions with higher unemployment levels. parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Italian government in order to reduce youth unemployment. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• A suitable policy to reduce youth unemployment could be a targeted vocational training and education program aimed at improving the skills of young workers in the southern regions.  
• “Youth unemployment” is the share of the labor force aged approximately 15–24 that is jobless but actively seeking work, and “structural unemployment” is long-term unemployment resulting from institutional and technological changes that mismatch skills with vacancies.  
• According to supply-side economic theory, strengthening human capital raises labor productivity, shifting the long-run aggregate supply curve to the right and lowering structural unemployment. Training programs would reduce mismatches by ensuring the young workforce acquires industry-relevant skills.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2c8455d5-d520-42a2-9fbf-92887197e7f4)
• In the context of Italy’s data, high public debt (145% of GDP) poses limitations on major government spending, but a well-targeted vocational program can be more cost-effective compared to large-scale fiscal expansion, focusing on equipping the unemployed youth with transferable skills.  
• Greater employability of youths in the southern regions would mitigate regional disparities in income, aligning with the concern over the Gini coefficient that remains relatively high (0.33–0.34). Reducing unemployment would also help stabilize consumption and avoid the social costs of long-term joblessness.  
• This policy can be combined with incentives for firms to hire trainees and apprentices (such as temporary tax breaks or lower social security contributions), which could reduce firms’ costs. In turn, active labor market policies can channel these new skills into productive employment.  
• Formal education improvements, expansion of vocational schools, and partnerships with private industry would elevate the quality of human capital, matching the needs of the Italian economy’s industrial base and boosting competitiveness globally.  
• Although costs of implementing training programs may create an opportunity cost due to Italy’s high public debt, long-term benefits include lower unemployment benefits, higher tax revenue from employed youth, and reduced income inequality.  
• Considering the data on persistent youth unemployment, the anticipated result of this policy is a sustainable reduction in structural unemployment, especially in the southern regions, stimulating balanced economic growth.  
• Therefore, the recommended policy is to invest in comprehensive vocational training and educational initiatives for youth, supported by hiring incentives, as a targeted and more fiscally manageable approach to reducing youth unemployment in Italy.

questions.specification:
**Tourism Industry and Externalities in Thailand**

Thailand's tourism sector has been a cornerstone of its economic growth, contributing approximately 20% to GDP and employing millions directly and indirectly. However, the explosive growth in tourism, particularly in destinations like Bangkok, Phuket, and Chiang Mai, has created significant challenges. These include severe overcrowding in urban centers, environmental degradation in natural attractions like Maya Bay (which had to be closed for rehabilitation), and skyrocketing property prices in popular tourist areas like Pattaya and Koh Samui.

The Thai government implemented a tourist tax in 2024 to address these negative externalities and increased funding for conservation projects through the Tourism Authority of Thailand (TAT). The tax aims to support sustainable tourism development and protect Thailand's natural resources.

Despite these initiatives, Thai businesses in the hospitality sector argue that the tourist tax reduces their competitiveness compared to other Southeast Asian destinations like Vietnam and Indonesia. Critics, including environmental NGOs and local community groups, contend that insufficient portions of the tourism revenue are directed toward addressing fundamental environmental issues, such as Bangkok's waste management crisis and inadequate urban planning in rapidly growing tourist destinations.

Thailand's membership in ASEAN has significantly influenced its trade patterns, particularly through the ASEAN Free Trade Area (AFTA). Thai exports, especially in the automotive and electronics sectors, have grown substantially, while agricultural imports have increased. While this has generally improved Thailand's trade balance, the country's growing dependence on food imports has raised concerns about long-term food security in what was historically known as "Asia's rice bowl."

Thailand's current account has strengthened due to rising export revenues, particularly in manufacturing. However, economic analysts warn that the substantial FDI inflows, especially in the Eastern Economic Corridor (EEC), could lead to significant profit repatriation by multinational corporations, potentially impacting Thailand's income balance in future years.

parts.content: Using a market failure diagram, explain how the tourist tax can help correct the negative externalities caused by tourism.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/66b325ba-4539-49be-8f8b-2d93f23333ce)

- Initially, the market consumption is above the social optimal level at price $P_m$ and quantity $Q_m$ where the Marginal Private Benefit exceeds that of the Social Benefit, indicating an external cost (see part 4)
- If the tax is imposed on producers, this will cause the cost of production to increase by the tax per unit, shifting the $MPC$ curve by an amount equivalent to the tax. 
- Hence the intersection of the demand curve and the supply curve with tax determines the price that consumers will pay for the good at $P_c$ which is higher than market, while producers will earn without the taxes at $P_{opt}$ (assuming that the tax was equivalent to the externality, otherwise the producer price would be between $P_m$ and $P_{opt}$ likely.)
- This caused a decrease in overall consumption, and thus the quantity falls from $Q_m$ to $Q_{opt}$ showcasing that a tax equivalent to that of the external cost could potentially erase the welfare loss caused by the externality.

- Higher Foreign Investment: A strong commitment to sustainability can attract environmentally conscious investors and businesses, particularly in eco-tourism and green technology sectors, further strengthening Thailand’s FDI inflows.

- Job Creation: Environmental conservation projects, such as reforestation, waste management, and renewable energy initiatives, create employment opportunities, contributing to the declining unemployment rate (from 1.2% in 2020 to 0.9% in 2024).

- Resilience Against Climate Change: Thailand, being vulnerable to climate-related risks like rising sea levels and extreme weather events, can benefit from conservation spending to mitigate future economic disruptions and protect key industries like agriculture and tourism.

<marks>Maximum 4 marks overall</marks>
